The argument is from experience: because Christ has genuinely suffered under the same pressures that bear on his people, he is qualified—not merely willing—to help them. Calvin reads "being tempted" as meaning "being tried by our evils"—the word πειρασθείς denotes experience, probation, the real encounter with what tests and presses. Ellicott unpacks the chain: through the temptations Christ faced arose opportunities for sin; in resisting them and suffering through them he gained not only sympathy but also knowledge—he knows exactly what help is needed and has the priestly position to provide it. Clarke, appealing to classical sentiment, cites the maxim that those who have endured most affliction feel most for others; Christ's suffering was not incidental to his compassion but constitutive of it. Wesley cuts to the proof: Christ's ability to succor the tempted is a "manifest, demonstrative proof"—the fact itself, once grasped, needs no further argument. Robertson notes the perfect tense of πέπονθεν ("hath suffered"): the experience of suffering is a permanent part of Christ's history as the exalted Lord, not a past event that has faded. The empathy is not remembered; it is retained.

voices.html: The necessity of being "in all things made like to His brethren" is now illustrated from the result. Through the temptations arose those conditions which required a High Priest's intercession; and in his having been tempted lies his special ability to help the tempted—by his sympathy, by his knowledge of what help is needed, and by the position of High Priest which he has gained through suffering.

A state of suffering disposes persons to be compassionate, and those who endure most afflictions are they who feel most for others. The apostle argues that it was necessary that Jesus Christ should partake of human nature exposed to trials, persecutions, and various sufferings, that he might the better feel for and be led to succour those who are afflicted and sorely tried.

Hath suffered (πέπονθεν)—second perfect active indicative; the suffering is a permanent part of Christ's experience. Being tempted (πειρασθείς)—the temptation to escape the Cross was early and repeatedly presented: in the wilderness, through Peter, in Gethsemane. He is able to help (δύναται βοηθῆσαι)—the present tense of ability grounds the ongoing intercession of the exalted High Priest.

For in that he hath suffered being tempted himself, he is able to succour them that are tempted—that is, he has given a manifest, demonstrative proof that he is able so to do. The suffering and the succouring are not two separate things: the suffering through temptation is precisely what equips him to be the helper of the tempted.

1.synthesis:
The writer opens his formal comparison of Christ with the Levitical high priesthood by stating the essential characteristics of the Aaronic office. Every high priest is taken from among men — not from angels, not from a superior order, but from the same human stock as those he represents before God. Calvin notes that this humanity is not incidental but structural: it ensures that the priest acts not for himself privately but for the whole people. He is ordained for men in things pertaining to God — Wesley captures this economically as one who brings God near to men and men to God. The office requires both gifts (unbloody offerings) and sacrifices (blood-offerings), Ellicott observing that on the Day of Atonement both categories address sins.

Clarke traces the phrase "taken from among his brethren" to Leviticus 21:10, grounding the requirement in Torah itself. Barnes reads this as the epistle's foundational apologetic move: the Jewish reader who had forsaken the Mosaic priesthood needed to see that the Christian system possessed a High Priest at least equal in every respect — and surpassing him in every qualification the writer will unfold.

voices.html: He compares Christ with the Levitical priests, and teaches us what is the likeness and the difference between them. He first says that the priests were taken from among men; secondly, that they did not act a private part but for the whole people; thirdly, that they were not to come empty to appease God, but furnished with sacrifices; fourthly, that they were not to be exempt from human infirmities.

The former term (gifts) is in itself perfectly general; but when contrasted with "sacrifices" it denotes the unbloody offerings of the Law. On the Day of Atonement, which is almost always in the writer's thoughts as he refers to the functions of the high priest, all offerings, as well as all sacrifices, had relation to sins.

For every high priest taken from among men — This seems to refer to Leviticus 21:10, where it is intimated that the high priest shall be taken from his brethren; that is, he shall be of the tribe of Levi, and of the family of Aaron. He is appointed to preside over the Divine worship in those things which relate to man's salvation.

The Jews regarded the office of high priest as an essential feature in the true religion; and it became, therefore, of the highest importance to show that in the Christian system there was a High Priest every way equal to that of the Jews — in rank, in character, and in the sacrifice which he offered — more than equal to the Jewish high priest.

2.synthesis:
The high priest's capacity for compassion (μετριοπαθεῖν) with the ignorant and erring flows from his own participation in human infirmity. Calvin draws on the full Greek philosophical background: the Stoics demanded complete apathy toward passion, while the Platonists counseled moderate passion — and the apostle chooses the Platonic term to describe a priest who neither turns a blind eye to sin nor falls into harshness. Clarke unpacks the word as meaning to act with moderation, to pity and feel for others, to excuse as far as possible, and when punishment must come, to administer it gently. The compassion is calibrated, not indiscriminate.

Wesley notes that the compassion is proportionate to the offense and distinguishes between "the ignorant" (those in error) and "the wandering" (those in sin) — two overlapping but distinct categories of failure. Both Ellicott and Barnes stress that the qualification cuts both ways: apathy or undue severity would equally disqualify the representative of men before God. The priest must stand in the middle: close enough to human weakness to feel it, principled enough not to excuse it.

voices.html: The classic use of μετριοπαθεῖν: the Stoics maintained that a wise man should be ἀπαθής, not subject to passions; the Platonists averred that he should μετριοπαθεῖν — regulate his passions moderately. The Apostle refers to this latter meaning: that the priest ought to be kind and gentle to sinners, because he partakes of their infirmities.

Rather, as one who can deal gently with the ignorant and erring, because he himself is compassed with infirmity. Either apathy or undue severity in regard to transgression would disqualify this representative of men to God. The language is so chosen as to exclude sinning with a high hand.

The word μετριοπαθεῖν signifies not merely to have compassion, but to act with moderation, to bear with each in proportion to his ignorance, weakness, and untoward circumstances — in a word, to pity, feel for, and excuse as far as possible; and when punishment must be administered, to do it in the gentlest manner.

Who can have compassion — in proportion to the offence; so the Greek word signifies. On the ignorant — them that are in error. And the wandering — them that are in sin. Seeing himself also is compassed with infirmity — even with sinful infirmity; and so needs the compassion which he shows to others.

3.synthesis: Because the Aaronic high priest is himself encompassed with infirmity, the law requires him to offer sacrifice not for the people alone but for himself as well. This is not incidental liturgical detail: it is structural to the Old Testament economy. Ellicott connects the verse directly to the law of the Day of Atonement (Lev 9:7), on which the high priest confessed sins first over a bull for himself and his household, then over the goats for the people — the sequence itself encoding the priest's personal need for atonement. Clarke notes that this very requirement teaches the priest compassion; knowing he too is a transgressor unable to keep the law in its spirituality, he is compelled toward fellow-feeling with others. Barnes makes the devotional application explicit: ministers of religion who preach the atonement must also plead the merits of that same blood for their own salvation. The Aaronic pattern thus underscores both the necessity of the sacrifice and the priest's solidarity with those he serves.

voices.html: The law of the Day of Atonement required a sin-offering of a bullock and a burnt-offering of a ram for the high priest himself, and for the congregation a sin-offering of two he-goats and a burnt-offering of a ram. Over his own sin-offering the high priest made confession of sins, first for himself and his household, then for the priests.

As he is also a transgressor of the commands of God, and unable to observe the law in its spirituality, he must offer sacrifices for sin, not only for the people, but for himself also: this must teach him to have a fellow feeling for others.

He is a sinner — an imperfect man. As for the people, so also for himself, to offer for sins. He needs the same atonement; he offers the sacrifice for himself which he does for others. The same thing is true of the ministers of religion now — they come before God feeling that they have need of the benefit of the same atonement which they preach to others.

4.synthesis:
The divine call, not personal ambition, is what legitimates the priestly office. No one may take this honor to himself; he must be called by God as Aaron was — designated by name, chosen by sovereign appointment, not by inheritance alone in the later corrupt sense. Calvin identifies this as the key structural point: what makes an office lawful is God's call, and both Aaron and Christ share this feature. The difference lies in what follows: Aaron's call established a temporary and transferable office, while Christ's established a perpetual one. Wesley adds a sharp observation often overlooked: Aaron was never a preacher; preaching was no part of the priestly office, a distinction vital for understanding what the writer means by Christ as priest.

Clarke grounds the argument in Israel's history — God specified the tribe and family, so human appointment has no standing here. Barnes notes the irony of his own day: by Paul's time the high priesthood had become venal, sold by Roman rulers, the divine call of the Mosaic law openly flouted. Against that corruption, the epistle's insistence on divine appointment for Christ is doubly pointed.

voices.html: There is to be noticed partly a likeness and partly a difference. What makes an office lawful is the call of God; so that no one can rightly and orderly perform it without being made fit for it by God. Christ and Aaron had this in common, that God called them both; but they differed in this, that Christ succeeded by a new and different way and was made a perpetual priest.

God himself appointed the tribe and family out of which the high priest was to be taken, and Aaron and his sons were expressly chosen by God to fill the office of the high priesthood. As God alone had the right to appoint his own priest for the Jewish nation, so God alone could provide and appoint a high priest for the whole human race.

No one taketh this honour — the priesthood — to himself, but he that is called of God, as was Aaron and his posterity, who were all of them called at one and the same time. But it is observable, Aaron did not preach at all; preaching being no part of the priestly office.

There were fixed and definite laws in regard to the succession in the office of the high priest, and to the qualifications of him who should hold the office. In the time of Paul these rules were little regarded; the office had become venal, and was conferred at pleasure by the Roman rulers.

5.synthesis:
The writer now applies the principle of divine call to Christ. Christ did not glorify himself to be high priest; he received the office from the Father, who said to him in Psalm 2:7, "Thou art my Son, this day have I begotten thee." Calvin explains that the "begetting" spoken of in the Psalm is not the eternal relation of the Son to the Father but the Father's public testimony to him before men — a manifestation of his sonship in history. The question is not who Christ is in himself but what kind of Son God manifested to the world: one invested with Mediatorial authority.

Ellicott presses the significance of "the Christ" (τὸν Χριστόν with the article): in the Pentateuch the high priest is often designated "the anointed priest," so in the single title "the Christ" the two testimonies of the Psalm converge — he is the Anointed King of Psalm 2 and the eternal Priest of Psalm 110. Clarke draws the anti-sacerdotal implication: if Christ's priesthood is divine in origin and eternal in duration, he can have no successors, no vicars, and no deputies either in heaven or on earth.

voices.html: The begetting of Christ, of which the Psalmist speaks, was a testimony which the Father rendered to him before men. What sort of Son did God manifest to us? One indued with no honor, with no power? Nay, one who was to be a Mediator between God and men. Thus he who was the eternal Son of God became our priest.

It is important to note that in passages of the Pentateuch where the high priest receives a special designation his title is almost always "the anointed priest." Hence in the one designation, "the Christ," are united the two testimonies of Scripture which follow: he is the Anointed King (Psalms 2:7), addressed by Jehovah as His Son; by the same Jehovah He is addressed as Priest for ever after the order of one who was both priest and king (Psalms 110:4).

Christ glorified not himself — the man Jesus Christ was appointed by God to the most awful yet glorious office, of being the High Priest of the whole human race. Rising from the dead, he ascended to heaven, and there ever appeareth in the presence of God for us. He can have no vicars, either in heaven or upon earth; those who pretend to be such are impostors.

The meaning is that Jesus was not ambitious; he did not intrude himself into the great office of high priest; he did not enter upon its duties without being regularly called to it. He received his appointment from the Father. This was decisive in the case; and this was sufficient, if it could be made out that he had this appointment.

6.synthesis:
The second proof-text for Christ's priestly office is Psalm 110:4: "Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec." Calvin calls this a remarkable passage from one of the clearest messianic psalms — one the Jews cannot evade without extreme effrontery, since the Psalm's opening verse addresses one greater than David, whom David himself calls Lord. Clarke draws out the force of "for ever": since Christ's priesthood is eternal, there can be no succession of priests, no sacerdotal hierarchy, no need of a vicar. Ellicott notes that Psalm 110:4 is the kernel of the whole Psalm and will supply the epistle's theme through chapter 7.

Barnes confirms that the messianic reading of the Psalm was not controversial: Paul argues as if no one would deny it, because at that time the Psalm was universally admitted by Jews and Christians alike to refer to the Messiah. The phrase "after the order of Melchisedec" is left unexplained here, reserved for the extended argument of chapter 7; but its mere citation is enough to signal that Christ's priesthood operates on a different and superior principle than Aaron's — not Levitical lineage but divine declaration.

voices.html: This is a remarkable passage, and indeed the whole Psalm from which it is taken; for there is scarcely anywhere a clearer prophecy respecting Christ's eternal priesthood and his kingdom. The Jews try all means to evade it, in order that they might obscure the glory of Christ; but they cannot succeed — for the Psalm's own opening forbids its application to David.

Psalm 110:4, here quoted, is the kernel of the Psalm, and supplies the theme for a large portion of this Epistle, especially chapter 7. As the promise of 2 Samuel 7 was the prelude to the revelation of the second Psalm, the divine declaration recorded in Exodus 19:6 may have prepared the way for the promise of Psalms 110:4.

Thou art a priest for ever — as long as the sun and moon endure, Jesus will continue to be high priest to all the successive generations of men. If he be a priest for ever, there can be no succession of priests; and if he have all power in heaven and in earth, he can have no vicars; nor can the Church need one to act in his name.

Paul makes much of this argument in a subsequent part of this epistle, Hebrews 7, and reasons as if no one would deny that the Psalm had a reference to the Messiah. It is clear, from this, that the Psalm was understood by the Jews at that time to have such a reference, and that it was so universally admitted that no one would call it in question.

7.synthesis:
Hebrews 5:7–10 forms the experiential core of the priestly argument: the passage demonstrates that Jesus possessed the compassion and solidarity with human weakness that qualifies a high priest, not merely by birth but by suffering. Verse 7 describes the scene of Gethsemane — "the days of his flesh," as Clarke calls the whole period of the incarnation — when Christ offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears to the one who was able to save him from death, and was heard for his reverent fear (εὐλαβεία).

Calvin identifies two causes of Christ's suffering here: the proximate (to learn obedience) and the ultimate (to be consecrated as priest for our salvation). Clarke calls verse 7 "one of the most difficult places in this epistle," noting that the apostle may be referring to something in the agony that the evangelists did not distinctly mark. Wesley offers a vivid summary: Christ prayed thrice with strong crying and tears, was heard in "that which he particularly feared" — not death itself but the totality of judgment it entailed — and endured it in obedience to the Father's will. Barnes ties verse 7 back to the priestly qualification of 5:2: by suffering so deeply, Christ acquired the capacity for compassion with those who suffer.

voices.html: The Apostle teaches us that Christ's wonderful goodness shines forth especially in this respect, that he for our good subjected himself to our infirmities. He points out two causes why it behooved Christ to suffer: the proximate was that he might learn obedience; and the ultimate, that he might be thus consecrated a priest for our salutation.

This is one of the most difficult places in this epistle, if not in the whole of the New Testament. It is probable that the apostle refers to something in the agony of our Lord which the evangelists have not distinctly marked. Who in the days of his flesh — the time of his incarnation, during which he took all the infirmities of human nature upon him, and was afflicted in his body and human soul just as other men are, irregular and sinful passions excepted.

Who in the days of his flesh — those two days, in particular, wherein his sufferings were at the height. Having offered up prayers and supplications — thrice. With strong crying and tears — in the garden. To him that was able to save him from death. And being heard in that which he particularly feared — when the cup was offered him, he prayed conditionally against it; and was heard.

The object of this verse and the two following is, to show that the Lord Jesus had that qualification for the office of priest referred to in Hebrews 5:2 — the ability to show compassion. In the days of his flesh he suffered intensely; he prayed with fervour; he placed himself in a situation where he learned by experience what it was to endure trial.

8.synthesis:
"Though he were a Son, he learned obedience by the things which he suffered." This verse is the hinge of the priestly argument: it establishes that the Son's suffering was neither accidental nor merely exemplary but constitutive of his qualification as priest. Calvin insists the learning was not remedial — Christ was not disobedient before he suffered, nor did he suffer to tame some ferocity of will. Rather, by enduring the cross, Christ manifested in deed the obedience whose disposition he already possessed, and in doing so exhibited to us the supreme instance of self-denial. Ellicott, following Lünemann, states this precisely: "The progress from the disposition to the deed of obedience is a practical learning of the virtue of obedience."

Wesley catches the literary elegance — the word "learned" premised to "suffered" elegantly shows how willingly he learned — and Barnes presses the theological precision: it cannot be supposed that Christ did not know what obedience was, or that he had perversities requiring to be subdued. He learned it experimentally and practically, becoming personally acquainted with the nature of obedience in the midst of protracted woes, as Philippians 2:8 describes. The phrase "though he were a Son" is Wesley's key pastoral note: it stands guard against any who would be offended at these evidences of human weakness.

voices.html: The proximate end of Christ's sufferings was to habituate himself to obedience — not that he was driven to this by force, or had need of being thus exercised as oxen or horses when their ferocity is to be tamed, for he was abundantly willing. But this was done from a regard to our benefit, that he might exhibit to us an instance and example of subjection even to death itself.

Though He was a Son, He learnt His obedience by the things which He suffered. The disposition of obedience Jesus possessed before He suffered, but the proof that this disposition existed must be shown in deed; this progress from the disposition to the deed of obedience is a practical learning of the virtue of obedience.

The word learned, premised to the word suffered, elegantly shows how willingly he learned. He learned obedience when he began to suffer; when he applied himself to drink that cup: obedience in suffering and dying. Though he were a Son — this is interposed, lest any should be offended at all these instances of human weakness.

He learned it experimentally and practically. It cannot be supposed that he did not know what obedience was, or that he was indisposed to obey God before he suffered, or that he had perversities of nature requiring to be subdued by suffering — but that he was willing to become personally and practically acquainted with the nature of such obedience in the midst of protracted woes.

9.synthesis:
"And being made perfect, he became the author of eternal salvation to all them that obey him." This verse names the telos of Christ's suffering: it was not merely educational (learning obedience) but inaugural — a solemn consecration into his priestly office. Calvin calls the cross a "kind of consecration" by which Christ was initiated into the priesthood, making the endurance of suffering not detrimental to his dignity but constitutive of his glory. Clarke reads τελειωθείς as "having obtained the goal" or "having finished" — referring to the completion of death and resurrection together — citing parallel usage in Hebrews 12:23. Wesley summarizes crisply: brought through all to glory by his sufferings.

Barnes explains why the language of "perfection" is not a comment on Christ's moral standing (he was always without sin) but on the completion or fitness of his character as Savior — all the requisite sufferings accomplished, all the qualifications assembled. The phrase "to all them that obey him" is significant: both Wesley and Barnes note that salvation is not universal but conditioned on obedience, echoing the structural point that priestly access requires response from those served.

voices.html: Here is the ultimate or remoter end, why it was necessary for Christ to suffer: it was that he might thus become initiated into his priesthood. The enduring of the cross and death were to Christ a solemn kind of consecration, by which he intimates that all his sufferings had a regard to our salvation. Thus they are so far from being prejudicial to his dignity that they are on the contrary his glory.

Τελειωθεὶς — and having finished all, having died and risen again. The word signifies to have obtained the goal, to have ended one's labor. When Christ had finished his course of tremendous sufferings, and consummated the whole by his death and resurrection, he became the cause of eternal salvation unto all them who obey him.

And being perfected — by sufferings, Hebrews 2:10; brought through all to glory. He became the author — the procuring and efficient cause — of eternal salvation to all that obey him — by doing and suffering his whole will.

Being made a complete Saviour — fitted in all respects to redeem men. Sufferings were necessary to the completeness of his character as a Saviour — not to his moral perfection, for he was always without sin. He became the Author — the procuring cause — of salvation. Only those who obey him have any evidence that they will be saved.

10.synthesis:
The writer closes the paragraph by restating the priestly designation from Psalm 110:4 — Christ is "called" (more precisely, addressed or greeted) of God a high priest after the order of Melchisedec. Ellicott notes that the Greek word (προσαγορευθείς) does not mean appointment in the active sense but being formally addressed as such, the way one greets a king at his coronation. Clarke's note on the same root (Hesychius: "to salute") confirms this — those who saw a man anointed king would cry "Hail, king!" In that sense, Psalm 110:4 is God's solemn salutation of Christ in his priestly office.

Ellicott also observes that the repetition of 5:6's words at the close of this paragraph is "singularly impressive" and marks the end of the doctrinal statement. Everything from 5:11 to 6:20 will be digression — a pastoral reproof, warning, and encouragement before the argument about Melchisedec resumes in chapter 7. Calvin and Barnes read the verse as a pivot: the claim that Christ is high priest after this order is so weighty that the writer must pause to ensure his readers are spiritually capable of receiving it.

voices.html: Rather, addressed. The divine words are, "Thou art a priest for ever." This repetition of the words of verse 6 at the close of the paragraph is singularly impressive. At this point the course of the argument is interrupted by a long digression (5:11 to 6:20), to which the writer is led by reflection on the inability of his readers to receive the teaching which befits their Christian standing.

Προσαγορευθεὶς — being constituted, hailed, and acknowledged to be a high priest. In Hesychius we find προσαγορεύει translated ἀσπάζεται — hence we learn that one meaning of this word is to salute; as when a man was constituted or anointed king, those who accosted him would say, Hail king!

Called of God — addressed by him, or greeted by him. The word here used does not mean that he was appointed by God in the sense in which we often use the word, but simply that he was addressed as such, to wit, in Psalm 110. Whether the word priest or high priest be used here does not affect the argument of the apostle.

11.synthesis:
The writer interrupts the doctrinal argument about Melchisedec to diagnose a pastoral problem: he has much to say, but they are hard of interpretation — not because the subject is obscure by nature, but because they have become "sluggish in hearing" (Ellicott). Calvin draws the pointed distinction: the difficulty is not in the subject matter but in themselves. The Lord speaks clearly, and his word is rightly called our light; but its brightness becomes dim through our own darkness. Wesley connects this to the upcoming reproof, identifying the preachers of the gospel as having many things to say, obscured by the hearers' sloth.

Clarke discusses the ambiguity of "of whom" (περὶ οὗ) — whether it refers to Melchisedec specifically or to Christ and his endless priesthood — and concludes the latter is more probable, since the difficulty in chapter 7 is no greater than the difficulty here. Barnes notes the pastoral challenge: a young convert, or one regressing in maturity, cannot be expected to receive deep teaching about Melchisedec, and the same principle holds today. What the readers lack is not information but the spiritual formation that enables them to receive it.

voices.html: He states that the cause of the difficulty was not in the subject but in themselves. And indeed the Lord speaks to us so clearly and without any obscurity, that his word is rightly called our light; but its brightness becomes dim through our darkness. He reminds them that they were to prepare themselves lest these things should be said in vain.

Hard of interpretation, seeing ye have become sluggish in hearing. Their faculty of hearing had once been acute, and then few words and little explanation, even on such a subject as this, would have sufficed; now there has come upon them a lack of interest, and with this a want of power.

Concerning whom — the apostle here begins an important digression, wherein he reproves, admonishes, and exhorts the Hebrews. We — preachers of the gospel — have many things to say, and hard to be explained — though not so much from the subject-matter, as from your slothfulness in considering and dulness in apprehending the things of God.

Paul knew that what he had to say of this man, as a type of the Redeemer, would excite wonder, and that many might be disposed to call it in question. He knew that, in order to be understood, what he was about to say required a familiar acquaintance with the Scriptures, and a strong and elevated faith. One of the first questions which a young convert often asks is, Who was Melchisedek?

12.synthesis:
The rebuke sharpens: given the time they have been Christians, they ought to be teachers — not necessarily public preachers, Barnes clarifies, but people capable of explaining Christian truth to children, neighbors, and inquirers. Instead they need to be taught again the first principles of divine revelation, described as "the first rudiments" or, in Calvin's cutting phrase, the alphabet. To need to learn the alphabet after years in school is not merely slow progress; it is regression. The word "again" (πάλιν) carries all the pastoral irony.

Ellicott reads "the oracles of God" here as the Old Testament Scriptures — the same scriptures the Hebrew readers should have known intimately — and "strong meat" as those deeper truths of Christianity that explain and express its proper nature. Clarke takes the rudiments to be specifically the priestly prophecies of the Old Testament, the very texts the epistle is expounding. Calvin's summary is mordant: "You do not know the alphabet. We must learn through life; for he alone is truly wise who owns that he is not yet wise at all" — but to remain indefinitely at the alphabet is not humility; it is sloth.

voices.html: This reproof contains very sharp goads to rouse the Jews from their sloth. He says that it was unreasonable and disgraceful that they should still continue in the elements, in the first rudiments of knowledge, while they ought to have been teachers. He mentions "the first principles" as though he had said, You do not know the alphabet.

Taking into account the time that had elapsed since they became Christians. Ye have need that some one teach you again the rudiments of the beginning of the oracles of God — the Scriptures of the Old Testament. Their temptation apparently was towards mingling a rudimentary Christian doctrine with the teaching of the synagogue.

You have been Christians long enough to be expected to understand such doctrines. He does not mean to say that they ought all to become public teachers or preachers of the gospel, but that they ought to be able to explain to others the truths of the Christian religion — as parents to children, as neighbours to neighbours, as friends to inquirers.

13.synthesis:
The image of milk versus solid food distinguishes the spiritually immature from those ready for deeper teaching. But the commentators diverge meaningfully on what "milk" and "the word of righteousness" specifically denote. Clarke reads "milk" as the doctrines of the Mosaic law — the rudiments intended to lead to Christ — and "the word of righteousness" as the doctrine of justification by faith: those who rest in legal ceremony remain unskilled in justification. Wesley gives a complementary but distinct reading: those who desire and can digest nothing but the doctrine of imputed righteousness (justification alone) are the "babes" here — they lack the full range of gospel truth. Barnes takes the most common Reformed line: "babes" are not false Christians but Christians who lack the experience to receive the higher mysteries, still "partaking of milk" as their sole food.

Ellicott notes the subtle change from "needing milk" (5:12) to "partaking of milk" as sole food — the shift marks a self-inflicted limitation, not merely a stage of development. The verse indicts not ignorance of the advanced but willful attachment to the elementary.

voices.html: The change of expression from having need of milk to partaking of milk — that is, making it the sole food — is significant. Their temptation apparently was towards mingling a rudimentary Christian doctrine with the teaching of the synagogue. Yielding to this they would lose all real knowledge of the very elements of Christian truth.

It is very likely that the apostle, by using this term, refers to the doctrines of the law, which were only the rudiments of religion, and were intended to lead us to Christ, that we might be justified by faith. He that uses milk — rests in the ceremonies and observances of the law — is unskilful in the doctrine of justification; for this requires faith in the sacrificial death of the promised Messiah.

Every one that useth milk — that neither desires, nor can digest, anything else. Is unexperienced in the word of righteousness — the sublimer truths of the gospel. Such are all who desire and can digest nothing but the doctrine of justification and imputed righteousness.

It does not mean that they were not true Christians, but that they had not the experience or skill requisite to enable them to understand the higher mysteries of the Christian religion. Those who are addressed had lost interest in the deeper truths of Christianity, those truths which alone expressed and explained its proper nature.

13.key_tension: Clarke reads 'milk' as the Mosaic law and 'the word of righteousness' as the doctrine of justification; Wesley reads the babes as those fixated on imputed righteousness alone; Barnes reads them as genuine but immature Christians — three distinct diagnoses of the same spiritual condition.

14.synthesis:
Solid food belongs to the mature — those who by reason of habit have trained their senses to discern between good and evil. The word "habit" (ἕξις) is the key: spiritual maturity is not a given at conversion but developed by constant exercise of every faculty under the discipline of sound doctrine. Wesley defines the term precisely: "strength of spiritual understanding arising from maturity of spiritual age." Calvin frames it theologically: the mature are "spiritual" Christians — those who by sound doctrine are fortified against all falsehood and the delusions of Satan, equipped with what Paul calls "the sword of the Spirit." Without this discernment, believers remain vulnerable to every wind of doctrine (Eph 4:14).

Clarke enumerates the "strong food" of mature Christianity — atonement, justification by faith, the gift of the Holy Spirit, Christ dwelling in the soul, triumph over death, resurrection, and final glorification — and Barnes grounds maturity not in intelligence but in long experience: only those who have walked with God through trial can appreciate and apply these doctrines. The contrast with the previous verse is complete: babes subsist on the elementary; the mature thrive on the full range of revealed truth.

voices.html: He calls those perfect who are adults, in opposition to babes. Such he would have all Christians to be — spiritual people who have attained by continual practice a habit. For he cannot have been otherwise taught aright in the truth, except we are fortified by his protection against all the falsehoods and delusions of Satan. Paul points out this benefit conferred by sound doctrine when he says, "That we may not be carried about by every wind of doctrine" (Ephesians 4:14).

Strong meat — the high and sublime doctrines of Christianity: the atonement, justification by faith, the gift of the Holy Ghost, the fullness of Christ dwelling in the souls of men, triumph in and over death, the resurrection of the body, the glorification of both body and soul in the realms of blessedness. By reason of use — who by constant hearing, believing, praying, and obedience, use all the graces of God's Spirit, and find every one improved, so that they daily grow in grace.

Strong meat — these sublimer truths relating to perfection (Hebrews 6:1). Belong to them of full age, who by habit — habit here signifies strength of spiritual understanding, arising from maturity of spiritual age. By, or in consequence of, this habit they exercise themselves in these things with ease, readiness, cheerfulness, and profit.

Solid food pertains to those of maturer years. The meaning is that by long use and habit they had arrived to that state in which they could appreciate the more elevated doctrines of Christianity — by long experience, after having been long practiced in the Christian life, in self-examination, in prayer, in meeting trials, and in efforts to do good.

1.synthesis:
The exhortation to move on from first principles to maturity arises directly from the rebuke of 5:11–14. Calvin explains the metaphor precisely: one never ceases to need the foundation of a house, but to be always engaged in laying it — never building on it — is ridiculous labor. "Leaving" the first principles does not mean forgetting them but refusing to remain in them, pressing forward to the superstructure. Ellicott captures the urgency in the Greek: "let us press onwards unto perfection" (φερώμεθα) — a momentum word, not a settled resolve.

Wesley lists the foundational doctrines the writer has in mind: repentance from dead works, faith toward God, the doctrine of baptisms, laying on of hands, resurrection of the dead, and eternal judgment — six elements, two related to the past (repentance and faith), two to initiation rites (baptisms and ordination), and two to eschatology. The symmetry is pastoral: these are not arbitrary rudiments but the entire entry-level catechesis of the early church. The summons is not to abandon them but to build from them toward the full adulthood of the faith.

voices.html: He bids them leave these rudiments, not that the faithful are ever to forget them, but that they are not to remain in them. For in building a house we must never leave the foundation; and yet to be always engaged in laying it would be ridiculous. He who is occupied in laying the foundation and proceeds not to the superstructure wearies himself with foolish and useless labor.

Rather, let us press onwards unto perfection. There is an urgency in the words which is missed by the ordinary rendering. The word "perfection" (teleiotes) answers to that rendered "full grown" (teleios) in the preceding verse, and expresses maturity, full development — the Christian's final aim.

Therefore leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ — that is, saying no more of them for the present. Let us go on to perfection; not laying again the foundation of repentance from dead works, and faith in God — so St. Paul in his very first sermon at Lystra, Acts 14:15, Turn from those vanities unto the living God.

The design of this chapter seems to be, to warn them against the danger of going back entirely, and to encourage them to make the highest attainments possible in the knowledge of Christianity and in the divine life. The apostle would keep them from entire apostasy, and would excite them to make all the advances which they possibly could make.

2.synthesis:
"The doctrine of baptisms and of laying on of hands, and of resurrection of the dead, and of eternal judgment" — the second pair and the eschatological pair of the six foundational doctrines. The phrase "doctrine of baptisms" is among the most discussed in Hebrews. Calvin treats the clause as grammatical apposition — the entire list of foundations is "the doctrine of baptisms and laying on of hands," meaning these ritual acts are the doctrinal content of the catechesis. Ellicott reviews the alternatives carefully: "baptisms of teaching" (Christian baptism as distinctively a baptism with teaching, Matt 28:19) versus multiple kinds of baptism (Jewish lustrations vs. Christian baptism). He finds the plurality most likely refers to Jewish and Christian washings needing to be distinguished.

Clarke, following Owen, takes "baptism" in the broader sense of doctrine (Deut 32:2; Moses in 1 Cor 10:2), with the laying on of hands referring to the gift of the Holy Spirit. Barnes distinguishes the two baptisms straightforwardly as the baptism of water and the baptism of the Spirit, both elementary Christian instruction. The plural "baptisms" resists a simple reading; the tradition remains genuinely mixed on whether these are Jewish vs. Christian, water vs. Spirit, or doctrine vs. practice.

voices.html: Some read them separately, "of baptisms and of doctrine;" but I prefer to connect them as being in apposition: "Not laying again the foundation of repentance, of faith in God, of the resurrection of the dead, which is the doctrine of baptisms and of the laying on of hands." For what is the doctrine of baptism but what he mentions here — faith in God, repentance, judgment, and the like?

The meaning of these words has been much controverted. The whole question of baptism amongst the Jews of the Apostolic age is full of difficulty. We must surely regard it as most unlikely that the baptism specifically Christian would be marked as "baptism of teaching," as if this distinguishing feature needed to be expressed.

Doctrine is called baptism, Deuteronomy 32:2; hence the people are said to be baptized to Moses when they were initiated into his doctrines, 1 Corinthians 10:2. The baptism of John was his doctrine (Acts 19:3); the baptism of Christ was the doctrine of Christ. The other was the communication of the gifts of the Holy Ghost, Acts 1:5; and this alone is what is intended by the laying on of hands.

Since the Jews made much of various kinds of washings, which were called baptisms, it was important that the true doctrine on the subject should be stated as one of the elements of the Christian religion. There are two baptisms whose necessity is taught by the Christian religion — that of water and that of the Spirit — both of which were among the first truths taught to converts.

2.key_tension: The plural 'baptisms' is interpreted variously: Calvin sees the whole clause as apposition to the foundational list; Clarke and Wesley read 'baptism' as doctrine/Spirit-reception; Ellicott and Barnes see a distinction between Jewish lustrations and Christian baptism — the tradition is genuinely divided on the reference.

3.synthesis: "And this will we do, if God permit." The advance to maturity is not a human achievement but a divine gift. Calvin interprets the qualification not as any uncertainty about God's willingness but as a reminder of human dependence: progress in the Christian life is God's special gift, and it is not in any person's power to bound at once from starting point to goal whenever he pleases. Ellicott adds an ominous nuance: God himself may have appointed a case beyond which no further progress — not even a new laying of foundations — is possible; that case is about to be described in verses 4–6. Barnes reads the qualification more gently: if God spares their lives, health, and reason, continues the means of grace, and imparts his Holy Spirit, they will advance. Clarke's rendering is warmly confident: "God being my helper, I will teach you all the sublime truths of the Gospel." All agree that the phrase is not hesitation but trust — an acknowledgment that every spiritual advance depends on divine concurrence.

voices.html: A dreadful denunciation follows; but the Apostle thus fulminated, lest the Jews should indulge their own supineness and trifle with the favor of God. There ought not in this case to be any delay; for there will not always be the opportunity for making progress; it is not in man's power to bound at once, whenever he pleases, from the starting point to the goal; but progress in our course is the special gift of God.

And this will we do, if God permit. There may be some with whom it will be impossible to press on to maturity of teaching and of Christian experience. There is a case excepted by God Himself from all efforts of the Christian teacher; in this case, though nothing can avail except the laying of a new foundation of repentance, God has appointed no agencies by which such a foundation can be laid.

And this will we do — God being my helper, I will teach you all the sublime truths of the Gospel; and show you how all its excellences were typified by the law, and particularly by its sacrificial system.

This is not to be interpreted as if God was unwilling that they should make such advances; but it is a phrase used to denote their dependence on him — if he would spare their lives, their health, and their reason; if he would continue the means of grace, and would impart his Holy Spirit; if he would favour their efforts and crown them with success.

4.synthesis:
The passage from 6:4 to 6:6 is the most disputed in the epistle and among the most debated in the New Testament. It describes persons who were once enlightened, tasted the heavenly gift, became partakers of the Holy Spirit — and then fell away. The writer declares it impossible to renew them again to repentance. Verse 4 opens the description of these persons: "those who were once enlightened." Ellicott traces the word back to Hebrews 10:32 and notes that in both places it is applied generally to all who received the Christian faith — a comprehensive metaphor for conversion. The grammatical structure is important: the whole sentence hangs on the word "renew" in verse 6; everything from verse 4 onward describes the subject of that verb.

Calvin takes the position that the passage does not endorse the Novatian error (no pardon for the lapsed) but that the apostle intends genuine apostates — those wholly deprived of God's Spirit and given up to a reprobate mind. Clarke restricts the application even further: the text refers specifically to those who reject the entire Christian system and side with blaspheming Jews in calling Christ an impostor, thus rendering their salvation impossible by wilful and malicious rejection. Barnes sets out the two interpretive traditions honestly — whether these are true Christians who fall away, or merely awakened non-Christians — and declines to resolve the question in favor of either Calvinist or Arminian dogma, attending instead to the exegetical facts.

voices.html: This passage has given occasion to many to repudiate this Epistle, especially as the Novatians armed themselves with it to deny pardon to the fallen. But when the design of the Apostle is understood, it appears evident that there is nothing here which countenances so delirious an error. The apostle intends genuine apostates — those deprived of God's Spirit and given up to a reprobate mind.

It will be seen that the greater part of this long sentence is dependent on the word "renew" in verse 6: "It is impossible to renew again unto repentance those who were once..." The metaphor of enlightening is introduced again in Hebrews 10:32; in that passage it is applied generally to all who are addressed, and includes everything involved in the reception of the Christian faith.

These verses do not belong nor are applicable to backsliders of any kind. They belong to apostates from Christianity — to such as reject the whole Christian system, and join with the blaspheming Jews, call Christ an impostor, and vindicate his murderers in having crucified him as a malefactor; and thus they render their salvation impossible, by wilfully and maliciously rejecting the Lord that bought them.

This passage has given occasion to much controversy, and the opinions of commentators and of the Christian world are yet greatly divided in regard to its meaning. On the one hand, it is held that it describes those who have been awakened and enlightened and then fall back; on the other, that it refers to those who are true Christians and then apostatize. I shall endeavour, as well as I may be able, to state the true meaning of the passage without reference to any theory.

4.key_tension: Calvin and Clarke read the apostates as wilful total defectors from Christianity who join its enemies; Barnes carefully withholds judgment on whether they were true regenerate believers; the division maps substantially onto whether one reads the passage from a Calvinist or Arminian soteriology.

5.synthesis: The description of the apostates continues: they have "tasted the good word of God and the powers of the world to come." Ellicott's note on the Greek is precise: the construction suggests not just tasting the good word but tasting that God's word is good — a personal, experiential apprehension of its excellence. The "powers of the world to come" or "powers of a coming age" refer to the miraculous signs that accompanied the gospel proclamation (Heb 2:4), or, as Ellicott suggests, the foretaste of the age to come given by the Spirit. Clarke reads the verse from two angles: either the powers are the miracles confirming the gospel, or they are the spiritual joys that anticipate eternal glory. Wesley offers a developmental analogy: just as a child naturally born first sees light, receives nourishment, and partakes of the things of this world, so the one born of the Spirit sees the light, tastes the sweetness, and partakes of the things of the world to come. Barnes pushes back against the reading of these marks as evidence of non-Christians: "this is language which cannot be applied to an impenitent sinner." The paradox at the heart of the passage is that such rich experience of divine grace can apparently coexist with the kind of apostasy described in verse 6.

voices.html: There is a change of construction in the Greek which suggests that the words rather mean, tasted that God's word is good — tasted the excellence of God's word, and of the powers. God's word was spoken through the Lord (Hebrews 2:3); the Hebrew Christians had heard and received this word and had proved for themselves its excellence. (Comp. 1 Peter 2:3.)

Genuine believers have an appetite for the word of God; they taste it, and then their relish for it is the more abundantly increased. The powers of the world to come may refer to the stupendous miracles wrought in confirmation of the Gospel; or they may refer to the spiritual joys which are the earnest of future blessedness.

And have tasted the good word of God — have had a relish for, and a delight in it. Every child that is naturally born first sees the light, then receives proper nourishment, and partakes of the things of this world. In like manner, one born of the Spirit sees the light, tastes the sweetness, and partakes of the things of the world to come.

They had experienced the excellency of the truth of God; they had seen and enjoyed its beauty. This is language which cannot be applied to an impenitent sinner. He has no relish for the truth of God, sees no beauty in it, derives no comfort from it. It is only the true Christian who has pleasure in its contemplation.

5.key_tension: Barnes reads the language of tasting as necessarily implying genuine regenerate experience; Ellicott and Wesley are more circumspect about mapping the descriptions directly onto the Reformed categories of election and perseverance.

6.synthesis:
"And have fallen away" — the verse that has generated more theological heat than almost any other in the epistle. The translation question is immediate: the Greek (καὶ παραπεσόντας) is not conditional but participial — not "if they fall away" but "having fallen away." Clarke and Wesley both insist on this correction, Clarke noting that Beza inserted a "si" (if) without manuscript authority to protect the doctrine of perseverance. Wesley is direct: "here is not a supposition, but a plain relation of fact." Ellicott confirms that the ordinary conditional translation is "altogether incorrect."

Having fallen away, these persons cannot be renewed because they re-crucify the Son of God and put him to open shame — that is, their apostasy is not passive but active, a public repudiation of Christ. Calvin interprets this as the result of the Holy Spirit's withdrawal: deprived of God's Spirit and given up to a reprobate mind, apostates rush headlong into destruction, adding sin to sin until wholly hardened they despise God or in despair express hatred of him. Barnes allows that the verse is about "a case which, in fact, might never occur" — it is like saying "had a man fallen down a precipice, it would be impossible to save him" — without affirming that such a fall has actually happened to any of his readers.

voices.html: Though this seems hard, yet there is no reason to charge God with cruelty when any one suffers only the punishment of his own defection. Such are deprived, as they deserve, of God's Spirit and given up to a reprobate mind, so that being the slaves of the devil they rush headlong into destruction. The end of all apostates is that they cease not to add sin to sin until wholly hardened they despise God.

There is no doubt that the ordinary translation ("if they shall fall away") is altogether incorrect, the Greek admitting of one rendering only. The successive clauses have shown that all the marks of the divine working in and with His word (Hebrews 2:4) have been found in these men — and then they fell away.

Our translators, following Beza, who without any authority from ancient MSS. inserted in his version the word si (if), rendered this clause "If they fall away," that this text might not appear to contradict the doctrine of the perseverance of the saints. But as no translator has authority to add to or alter the text, the word should be omitted, and the clause translated "having fallen away."

Literally, "and having fallen away." It is not an affirmation that any had actually fallen away, or that in fact they would do it; but the statement is that on the supposition that they had fallen away, it would be impossible to renew them again — as if we should say, "had a man fallen down a precipice, it would be impossible to save him."

6.key_tension: Calvin, Clarke, and Wesley read the apostasy as real and wilful, entailing permanent abandonment by God; Barnes interprets the verse as describing a hypothetical impossibility — a case posited but not necessarily actual — differing on whether the impossibility of renewal is descriptive (these persons simply cannot be reached) or judicial (God has closed the door on them).

7.synthesis:
The agricultural parable grounds the preceding argument in a natural analogy: land that receives abundant rain and produces good fruit is blessed; land that receives the same rain and yields only thorns and briars is rejected and given over to burning. Calvin reads the parable as designed to excite timely progress — as earth must germinate the seed quickly or lose it, so the recipient of the gospel word must respond without delay. Ellicott's note on "drinks in" is precise: the land actively absorbs the rain (Deut 11:11), suggesting that the reception itself is a mark of the kind of soil. The owner tills the land (the preachers and teachers), God gives the blessing or the curse — but the soil's nature determines the outcome.

Clarke draws the application clearly: those who receive the rain of the Gospel and bring forth fruit continue to be cultivated; the apostates receive the same rain and produce nothing but corruption. Barnes confirms the design: to show the consequences of not making proper use of privileges. The parable does not resolve the prior theological debate (vv. 4–6) but illustrates its practical stakes — the same gift of grace can issue in blessing or in judgment, depending on the soil that receives it.

voices.html: This is a similitude most appropriate to excite a desire to make progress in due time; for as the earth cannot bring forth a good crop unless it causes the seed as soon as it is sown to germinate, so if we desire to bring forth good fruit, as soon as the Lord sows his word it ought to strike roots in us without delay.

Land which not only receives but also drinks in abundance of rain, in such a climate as is here thought of, must either bring forth herbage or be condemned as irretrievably barren. This clause is added to show that nothing is wanting on the part of the owner or of the tillers of the land.

As much as if he had said: In giving up such apostates as utterly incurable, we act as men do in cultivating their fields; for as the ground which drinketh in the rain waters it, brings forth fruit to compensate the toil of the tiller, and continues to be cultivated, God granting his blessing to the labors of the husbandman; so too —

The design of the apostle by this comparison is apparent — to show the consequences of not making a proper use of all the privileges which Christians have, and the effect which would follow should those privileges fail to be improved. If the earth absorbs the rain and produces an abundant harvest, it receives the Divine blessing. If not, it is cursed, or is worthless.

8.synthesis: The negative side of the parable: the same ground, having drunk in the same rain and received the same cultivation, bears only thorns and briars — it is "rejected" (ἀδόκιμος, worthless) and near to cursing, its end being burning. Ellicott corrects the translation: "if it bear thorns and briars" should be "but if it bear thorns and briars" — the condition is the same well-watered land, making the failure inexplicable from any natural cause, and thus leaving it with no recourse but judgment. Clarke's use of the Latin agricultural maxim ("Saepe etiam steriles incendere profuit agros") captures a common ancient practice: unproductive brushland is burned off. Wesley reads the "end is to be burned" as a prophetic allusion — as Jerusalem was burned shortly after. Barnes notes that "near to cursing" means not that a curse has been formally pronounced but that the land is devoted to destruction, given over as useless. The parable's force lies in the equal gift (rain) meeting unequal response (fruit vs. thorns), with the judgment reflecting not divine capriciousness but the soil's own failure despite every advantage.

voices.html: Rather, but if it bear thorns and briars it is rejected. If that very land which has drunk in the abundant rain and received careful culture still proves unfruitful, it is rejected. Man can do no more; and the curse of God is "near"; its end is "for burning" — probably found in Deuteronomy 29:23, the land of Sodom, "brimstone and salt and burning."

That which beareth thorns and briers is rejected — the land which, notwithstanding the most careful cultivation, receiving also in due times the early and latter rain, produces nothing but thorns and briers, is rejected (ἀδόκιμος), given up as unimprovable; its briers, thorns, and brushwood are burnt down, and then it is left to be pastured on by the beasts of the field.
That which beareth thorns and briers — only or chiefly. Is rejected — no more labour is bestowed upon it. Whose end is to be burned — as Jerusalem was shortly after.

That which beareth thorns and briers is rejected by the farmer — abandoned as worthless. The word cursing means devoting to destruction. The sense is not that the owner would imprecate a curse in words, but the language is taken from the more common use of the word curse — as meaning to devote to destruction. So the land would be given up to be overrun with fire.

9.synthesis:
After the severity of verses 4–8, the writer softens his tone — Calvin notes that this pastoral balance is essential: to address disciples in such a way as always to add encouragement rather than diminish it, since nothing alienates more from the truth than to see oneself deemed past hope. The address "beloved" (ἀγαπητοί) appears here for the only time in Hebrews, marking the transition from warning to encouragement. Wesley observes that the writer uses this appellation nowhere else in the epistle, and never uses it except in exhorting. The writer declares himself "persuaded of better things" — things that accompany salvation, not the state nigh to cursing but the state nigh to salvation.

Ellicott reads "the better things" as the alternative of 6:7 — fruitful land rather than barren — and notes that the writer does not directly say "I have heard of your fruitfulness" but implies it, then grounds his hope in the parable's promise. Barnes draws two conclusions: the apostle regarded them as sincere Christians, and he believed they would not in fact fall away. Clarke encourages: "you give proofs still that you have not, whatever others have done, departed from the living God."

voices.html: As the preceding sentences were like thunderbolts by which readers might have been struck dead, it was needful to mitigate this severity. Whosoever wishes to do good by teaching ought so to treat his disciples as ever to add encouragement rather than to diminish it; for there is nothing that can alienate us more from attending to the truth than to see that we are deemed to be past hope.

Literally, the better things — that is, the alternative spoken of in verse 7. He has not written in despair, but for warning only; believing that to them belongs, not the state which is "nigh unto a curse," but that which borders on salvation (Hebrews 5:9).

But, beloved — here he softens what he had before said; having given them the most solemn warning against apostasy, he now encourages them to persevere, commends the good that is in them, and excites them to watchfulness and activity. Things that accompany salvation — you give proofs still that you have not departed from the living God.

But, beloved — in this one place he calls them so; he never uses this appellation but in exhorting. We are persuaded of you things that accompany salvation — we are persuaded you are now saved from your sins; and that ye have that faith, love, and holiness, which lead to final salvation. Though we thus speak — to warn you, lest you should fall from your present steadfastness.

10.synthesis:
"For God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labor of love." The writer grounds his confident hope not in a sentimental optimism but in a theological fact: God's justice (δικαιοσύνη) is the guarantor that faithfully lived love will not go unrewarded. Calvin addresses the potential misreading directly: the apostle is not making works the cause of salvation or making God a debtor to human merit. He is rather observing that from good beginnings he hoped for a good end — the works of love are evidence of genuine grace at work, not the ground of justification. God's faithfulness to his own promise ("I will not forget") is at stake, not his obligation to human achievement.

Ellicott sharpens this: man's work cannot in itself merit reward from God, but God's own righteous promise (1 John 1:9) means that works done in his name shall receive reward — the righteousness at stake is not human merit but divine faithfulness to promise. Clarke notes that the best manuscripts omit "labor" from the phrase, leaving simply "your work and the love which ye showed toward his name" — the simplest reading. Barnes confirms that the evidence of true piety here is their ministry to the saints, performed when there was no hope of human recompense.

voices.html: These words signify as much as though he had said, that from good beginnings he hoped for a good end. The Apostle does not here speak avowedly of the cause of our salvation; therefore no opinion can be formed from this verse, as though salvation were due to our works. He builds his hope of their salvation on the gifts of God which he saw in them.

Man's work cannot in itself merit reward from God, but (1 John 1:9) the righteous God cannot neglect His own promise and law that such works shall receive reward. Herein lies his hope. The "fruit" consisted in brotherly love, but it was offered unto God; the bond of brotherhood was the joint relation to "His name."

God is only bound to men by his own promise: this promise he is not obliged to make; but, when once made, his righteousness or justice requires him to keep it; therefore, whatever he has promised he will certainly perform. Every good work must spring from faith in the name, being, and goodness of God; and every work that is truly good must have love for its motive, as it has God for its end.

God will do no wrong. He will not forget or fail to reward the endeavours of his people to promote his glory and to do good. The meaning is that by their kindness in ministering to the wants of the saints, they had given full evidence of true piety — deeds of benevolence when there was no hope of recompense, love being the only motive.

11.synthesis:
The writer's earnest desire is that each one of them show the same diligence — not the collective church generically, but each individual — to the full assurance of hope until the end. Wesley's treatment of "full assurance of hope" is the most theologically rich in the tradition: he distinguishes between the full assurance of faith (highest degree of evidence of present pardon) and the full assurance of hope (the same degree of divine evidence, given by the same immediate inspiration of the Holy Spirit, of persevering grace and of eternal glory). The two are not the same; hope is the forward gaze of faith, and its "full assurance" is a high attainment, not a possession of all believers at all times.

Calvin frames the connection between the two tables of the Law: the Hebrews have shown love (the second table, evidenced in their works), but their faith must correspond — they need the full certainty of hope toward God, not only active charity toward people. Ellicott reads "fulness of hope" as "full productiveness of hope" — the harvest of the anchor image to follow. Barnes grounds the attainment of full assurance in persevering effort: it comes by ardor, zeal, and the strenuous leading of a holy life, not by passive expectation.

voices.html: He now freely reminds them of what was still wanting in them, lest his courtesy should appear to have in it any flattery. "You have made your love evident; it remains, however, that your faith should correspond with it." Now, by these words the Apostle shows that there are two parts in Christianity which correspond with the two tables of the Law.

Rather, fulness (full productiveness) of hope (Hebrews 10:22). His desire is that the zeal which they have manifested in works of love may be directed toward the attainment of the full harvest of Christian hope — may be shown until the very end.

The full assurance of faith relates to present pardon; the full assurance of hope, to future glory. The former is the highest degree of divine evidence that God is reconciled to me in the Son of his love; the latter is the same degree of divine evidence of persevering grace and of eternal glory — so much, and no more, as faith every moment beholds with open face, so much does hope see to all eternity.

And we desire that every one of you — we wish that every member of the church should exhibit the same endeavour to do good, until they attain to the full assurance of hope. The full assurance of hope is to be obtained by a persevering effort to lead a holy life — the same strenuous endeavour, the same ardour and zeal.

12.synthesis:
The summons not to become sluggish is paired with its positive counterpart: become imitators of those who through faith and patience inherit the promises. Ellicott notes the return of the word "sluggish" from 5:11, where it described intellectual dullness; here it describes the Christian life more broadly — the dullness of mind leads to the torpor of will. Calvin's point on the power of example is acute: a naked truth set before us does not affect us as much as seeing it fulfilled in the person of Abraham; and Abraham is named not because he alone exemplified this faith but because his example is more illustrious than any other.

Wesley defines "inherited the promises" briefly but significantly: the promised rest, paradise — not a future category but something the fathers have already entered. Barnes gives the exhortation its sharpest practical edge: by diligent and strenuous effort only could these Hebrew Christians secure themselves from the danger of apostasy. The word "followers" (μιμηταί) means imitators — not admirers from a distance but those who pattern their lives after the perseverance of the saints. Chapter 11 will give them a gallery of such models; here Abraham alone is named in advance.

voices.html: To sloth he opposes imitation; it is then the same thing as though he said, that there was need of constant alacrity of mind. But it had far more weight, when he reminded them that the fathers were not made partakers of the promises except through the unconquerable firmness of faith; for examples convey to us a more impressive idea of things than naked truth.

Rather, that ye become not sluggish. The same word is used as in Hebrews 5:11, there applied to apprehension of truth, here to the Christian hope and life; if the truth be not welcomed, there will be no vigour in the life. Better, imitators — of men who, having lived in hope, passed to the actual possession of the promised blessings by means of faith and patience.
Inherited the promises — the promised rest; paradise.

By diligent and strenuous effort only could they secure themselves from the danger of apostasy. But followers — imitators — that you may live as they lived. Of them who through faith and patience — by confidence in God and patience in suffering — remaining faithful to God through trial, had been admitted to heaven.

13.synthesis:
The argument pivots to Abraham as the supreme example of faith and patient waiting, and to the oath God swore to him as the supreme example of divine confirmation. Calvin states the premise: the grace of God is offered in vain unless we receive the promise by faith and constantly cherish it in the bosom of the heart. Against every obstacle of nature — Sarah's barrenness, both their ages, every biological improbability — Abraham believed what God swore and was not disappointed. Ellicott identifies why Abraham is chosen for this argument: he is the most illustrious example of those who "inherit the promises"; the oath given to him is confirmed by oath; and in the promise to Abraham is latently contained the promise of the Christ (Gal 3:7, 29).

Clarke quotes Genesis 22:16–18 in full — the post-Moriah oath in which God swears by himself to bless and multiply Abraham — noting that the apostle's abbreviated quotation reflects his confidence that his readers know the Scriptures. Barnes offers two reasons for selecting Abraham: first, to show the nature of evidence Christians have (the same promise-and-oath structure), and second, because Abraham's perseverance over decades is exactly in point for readers tempted to falter after relatively brief trial.

voices.html: His object was to prove that the grace of God is offered to us in vain except we receive the promise by faith and constantly cherish it in the bosom of our heart. When God promised a countless offspring to Abraham, it seemed a thing incredible; Sarah had been through life barren; both had reached a sterile old age. Yet Abraham looked for this and feared no disappointment.

Abraham is chosen for special mention as the most illustrious example of those who "inherit the promises" (comp. John 8:58); also because (1) the assurance given to him was confirmed by oath; and (2) in it lay included the promise of the Christ. The promises made to Abraham were essentially one, with various parts progressively fulfilled.

When God made promise to Abraham — the promise referred to is that made when he had offered his son Isaac on the altar, Genesis 22:16–18: "By myself have I sworn, saith the Lord... that in blessing I will bless thee, and in multiplying I will multiply thy seed." He swore by himself because there was no greater by whom to swear.

Reference is made to Abraham in this argument probably for two reasons: (1) To show the nature of the evidence which Christians have that they will be saved — depending, as in Abraham's case, on the promise of God; and (2) because the example of Abraham was just in point — he had persevered, relied firmly and solely on the promise, and was rewarded with the fulfilment of the promise.

14.synthesis:
"Saying, Surely blessing I will bless thee, and multiplying I will multiply thee" (Gen 22:17). The doubled form — blessing I will bless, multiplying I will multiply — is a Hebrew idiom of absolute certainty and emphasis, and all commentators recognize it as such. Barnes notes that the same idiom appears throughout the Old Testament (Gen 16:10; Ex 8:10; Joel 3:14) and that it is equivalent to the most solemn form of affirmation. Clarke reads the promise as still being fulfilled: genuine Christians are Abraham's seed, and God is increasing their number daily.

Ellicott notices a subtle textual detail: the writer's quotation alters the end of the Genesis verse, changing "I will multiply thy seed" to simply "I will multiply thee." The effect is to concentrate attention on the blessing to Abraham himself — not only to his descendants — making the promise immediately personal for every reader who is Abraham's spiritual offspring. Wesley's comment is the economy of certainty: a single citation of the divine words, standing on their own authority.

voices.html: It will be observed that one change is made in the last word: for in Genesis we read, "I will multiply thy seed." The alteration may be made for brevity, as the quotation is abridged; but the effect of it is to direct greater attention to the first words and to fix the thought on the blessing promised to Abraham himself.

Saying, Surely blessing I will bless thee — I will continue to bless thee. Multiplying I will multiply thee — I will continue to increase thy posterity. In the most literal manner God continues to fulfil this promise; genuine Christians are Abraham's seed, and God is increasing their number daily.
Genesis 22:17.

Surely blessing I will bless thee — that is, I will certainly bless thee. The phrase is a Hebrew mode of expression, to denote emphasis or certainty — indicated by the repetition of a word. Multiplying I will multiply thee — I will greatly increase thee — I will grant thee an exceedingly numerous posterity.

15.synthesis:
"And so, after he had patiently endured, he obtained the promise." The key word is "patiently endured" (μακροθυμήσας): Abraham waited not weeks or months but decades, and his faith did not waver. Barnes reconstructs the chronology for the reader — Abraham was 75 when called and the promise was first given, then 24 more years elapsed before Isaac was born — so the "obtaining" came after nearly a quarter century of patient trust. Wesley's comment is the briefest possible and the most striking: "After he had waited — thirty years." Clarke draws the inference directly: if we believe and prove faithful unto death, we shall also inherit the promises; Isaac born supernaturally was the beginning of a fulfillment that climaxed in eternal glory.

Ellicott is careful to note that while some portions of the promise received partial fulfillment during Abraham's life, the writer does not have these partial fulfillments in view here. The "obtained the promise" refers not to the birth of Isaac alone but to the full inheritance of the promise that Abraham entered through faith, as Hebrews 11:13 makes clear: they died in faith, not having received the promises but having seen them from afar. Patient endurance is its own argument against the readers' temptation to give up too soon.

voices.html: And thus, having patiently waited, he obtained the promised gift. Though some portions of the promise received a partial accomplishment during Abraham's life, it is not this that the writer has in view. (See Hebrews 6:12, and Hebrews 11:13.)

He obtained the promise — Isaac was supernaturally born; and in his birth God began to fulfil the promise: while he lived, he saw a provision made for the multiplication of his seed; and, having continued steadfast in the faith, he received the end of all the promises in the enjoyment of an eternal glory.
After he had waited — thirty years. He obtained the promise — Isaac, the pledge of all the promises.

After he had patiently waited for a long time. He did not faint or grow weary, but persevered in a confident expectation of the fulfilment. When he was called by God and the promise of a numerous posterity was made to him, he was seventy-five years old; twenty-four years elapsed after this before the man of promise was born.

16.synthesis:
The logic of oaths among human beings: men swear by one greater than themselves, and an oath for confirmation settles all dispute. Calvin states the argument from the lesser to the greater — if credit is given to a man, who is by nature fallible, when he swears by God's name, how much more credit is due to God himself when he swears by himself? The argumentative structure is that oaths carry weight precisely because they invoke a third party greater than the swearer; God, who has no superior, swears by his own name, and thus his oath carries the weight of his entire being and veracity.

Wesley draws the notable implication that oaths taken in a religious manner remain lawful under the gospel — otherwise, he observes, the apostle would not have mentioned them with such honor as a proper means to confirm the truth. Clarke, following Macknight, concurs: both promissory oaths and oaths of confirmation, when required by proper authority and taken religiously, are allowable under the gospel dispensation. Barnes notes the social function: oaths end strife between parties at variance by introducing a solemn appeal to God that binds both parties.

voices.html: It is an argument from the less to the greater: if credit is given to man, who is by nature false, when he swears, and for this reason, because he confirms what he says by God's name, how much more credit is due to God, who is eternal truth, when he swears by himself?
Rather, and of every dispute in their case the oath is an end — is final — to settle the matter.

Men generally swear by him who is infinitely greater than themselves, and an oath for confirmation, to confirm what is promised or asserted, usually puts an end to all contradiction.
