Clarke finds this the key argumentative move: by stacking Genesis 2:2 and Psalm 95:11, the author demonstrates that God's rest is both ancient and still open — the oath is about entry into God's eternal Sabbath, not merely a land grant. Barnes observes that the logic runs: God rested at creation; the disobedient are excluded from that rest; the promise of entering that rest therefore remains for the faithful. Wesley sees in the dual quotation an invitation: the same oath that excludes the disobedient implicitly promises entry to the obedient — the rest is real and available to those who believe.

voices.html: The two quotations — Genesis 2:2 and Psalm 95:11 — are placed together to establish that the rest God swore the disobedient from is not merely Canaan but his own eternal Sabbath rest. Since God's Sabbath existed before Canaan was ever promised, and since the oath explicitly excludes those who disbelieve, the rest in view is something larger and more ancient than any earthly territory.

The conjunction of the two texts is the author's argumentative engine here. Genesis 2 establishes that God's rest is real and primordial. Psalm 95 says the disobedient cannot enter my rest. The only rest God can call his own is the Sabbath rest of creation; therefore the oath of Psalm 95 concerns entry into something as ancient and as real as the seventh day.

The author re-quotes the oath of exclusion immediately after establishing the Genesis rest, so that the reader will see: the rest from which the disobedient are excluded is the rest God himself entered at creation. That rest is still available — the oath only proves that entry requires faith, not that the rest itself is closed.

He quotes the oath again to keep the warning in view while the positive argument develops. The rest is real; God has it. The oath proves that some are excluded. But exclusion is by unbelief, which means inclusion is by faith. Both the promise and the warning are meant to move the reader toward the trust that opens the door.

6.synthesis: Seeing therefore it remaineth that some must enter therein, and they to whom it was first preached entered not in because of disobedience. The argument reaches a crucial inference: since God's rest exists and the first generation was excluded, it follows logically that the rest remains open — it remaineth that some must enter therein. Calvin presses the logic: God's purposes are not thwarted by human failure; if the wilderness generation did not fill the rest, the rest awaits another entry. The design of God cannot be frustrated. Ellicott observes that the phrase it remaineth (apoleipetai) is a key term the author will use again in verse 9 — it means remains over, is still outstanding, has not yet been claimed. The fact that some must enter is grounded in the character of God's promises: they do not return void. Barnes notes the logic of the excluded generation as proof that the promise survives them: their disobedience did not cancel the promise; it merely meant someone else would receive it. Clarke finds here one of the most hopeful arguments in the chapter: the failure of others does not close the door. The rest remains, and it remains for us if we will come to it with faith. Wesley applies this directly to any reader tempted to presume that the best opportunity has passed — the promise is still being offered, and today remains the day to respond.

voices.html: Since the wilderness generation was excluded, and since God's purpose cannot be permanently thwarted, it follows that the rest remains for others to enter. God does not make promises that go entirely unfulfilled. The exclusion of the disobedient is not the cancellation of the promise but its redirection toward those who will receive it with faith.

The word apoleipetai — it remains outstanding — will appear again in verse 9 as the basis for the Sabbath-rest argument. Here it establishes the logical premise: if some must enter (as the existence and design of the promise requires), and if the first generation did not (because of disobedience), then the entry is still open. The promise has not yet been fully claimed.

The exclusion of the first generation proves rather than disproves the availability of the rest. If the rest had been cancelled by their failure, the promise would have failed with it. Instead, their failure shows that the rest exists, that entry requires faith, and that the promise continues to stand for any who will receive it on those terms.

It remaineth that some must enter — God's counsel is not defeated by human unbelief. The rest is there; it will be filled; the only question is whether the readers will be among those who enter. The disobedience of the wilderness generation is a warning, not a doom pronounced on all their successors. The door is open; today is the day to walk through it.

7.synthesis: Again, he limiteth a certain day, saying in David, Today, after so long a time; as it is said, Today if ye will hear his voice, harden not your hearts. The argument now turns on chronology as evidence. Calvin observes the force of after so long a time: David wrote Psalm 95 centuries after Joshua's conquest of Canaan. If Joshua had fully given Israel the rest that the promise pointed to, there would be no reason for the Spirit to speak through David, long after the conquest, still urging Today, do not harden your hearts. The fact that David renewed the call to enter God's rest proves that the rest had not been permanently secured by Joshua. Ellicott notes the precise argument: the psalm was written in the Davidic era, when Israel was already in the land. If the land-rest were the final rest, the psalm would be meaningless. Its continued urgency points beyond the land to something not yet fully given. Barnes finds this among the most elegant arguments in the chapter: the Spirit through David quotes a warning about rest to people who are already in the promised land — which can only mean the rest in view is not the land. Clarke is struck by the Spirit's continuing urgency: even after centuries, even after the conquest, the call is still today, still do not harden your hearts — the rest the promise points to has not yet been fully realized. Wesley applies the logic: if you are in Canaan and still being called to enter God's rest, then God's rest is greater than Canaan.

voices.html: David's psalm was written after Joshua's conquest, long after the people were settled in the land. If Joshua had given them the rest the promise intended, there would be no reason for David — through the Spirit — to continue urging entry into God's rest. The psalm's continued urgency is the Spirit's own testimony that a greater rest remains, one that the Canaan settlement did not exhaust.

After so long a time — David writes centuries after Joshua. The land has been given; the nation is established. Yet the Spirit through David still calls: Today, harden not your hearts, that you may enter my rest. This temporal gap is the author's clinching argument: if Canaan were the ultimate rest, the Davidic psalm would be pointless. Its urgency proves a further rest is still outstanding.

The argument from David's era is decisive: if the land of Canaan were the final rest, then after Joshua settled the people in it, the call to enter God's rest would have been unnecessary. The fact that the Spirit through David continued to issue the call — centuries into the land period — proves that Canaan was a type, not the antitype. The real rest remained to be entered.

After so long a time — the long interval between Moses and David is itself an argument. The Spirit does not call people to enter what they already possess. The call in David's psalm, addressed to people who already lived in the promised land, can only refer to something they still lack: the true rest of God, which transcends every earthly territory.

8.synthesis: For if Jesus had given them rest, then would he not afterward have spoken of another day. The key to reading this verse is recognizing that Jesus in the Greek is the same word as Joshua — the author is speaking of Joshua son of Nun, not Jesus Christ. Calvin notes the elegant precision: the name is identical in Greek, which allows the point to be made sharply — the Jesus who led Israel into the land did not give the rest that God's promise was pointing toward. Ellicott explains the argument: if Joshua's conquest had given the final rest, God would not afterward — through David, centuries later — have spoken of another day for entering rest. The afterward is crucial: temporal sequence proves that Canaan-rest was preliminary. Barnes observes that this is the negative complement to the positive argument of verse 7: David's continued call proves a rest remains; Joshua's failure to give the final rest explains why the call continues. Clarke notes the peculiar richness of the name-coincidence: Joshua and Jesus share the same name, and Joshua's inability to give final rest points forward to the Jesus who will. The typological logic is complete: Joshua = type of the coming Jesus who gives the true rest. Wesley finds here both a historical argument and a Christological one: Joshua's rest was real but limited, pointing to the rest that only Jesus Christ can give.

voices.html: The Jesus here is Joshua — the names are identical in Greek. If Joshua had given the rest that God's promise intended, the Spirit would not afterward have spoken through David of another day and another entry. The fact that David's psalm came after Joshua's conquest proves that Joshua's work was preparatory, not final. It points to the true Jesus who gives what Joshua could only prefigure.

Joshua gave Israel Canaan, but Canaan was not the rest of God in its fullness. The word afterward is the argument: the Davidic psalm comes after Joshua, and it still speaks of entering rest as something yet to be achieved. Temporal sequence proves that Joshua's conquest did not fulfill the deepest intention of the promise. Another day, another entry, another rest remained.

If Joshua had given the complete rest, God would not through David have continued to call his people to enter it. The Davidic psalm presupposes that the rest is still outstanding. Joshua's rest was real, valuable, and promised by God — but it was the shadow. The substance, the true rest, awaited the coming of the greater Joshua, Jesus Christ.

Joshua and Jesus bear the same name in Greek, and the correspondence is not accidental. Joshua pointed to Jesus as a type to an antitype. What Joshua could give — the land — was a figure of what Jesus gives — true rest for the soul. The failure of Joshua's rest to satisfy the promise permanently is itself the testimony to something greater that the name-bearer would provide.

9.synthesis: There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God. This verse is the climax of the chapter's argument. The Greek word translated rest here is unique: sabbatismos — not the usual katapausis used throughout the chapter, but a word apparently coined by the author, appearing only here in the New Testament. Calvin notes the significance: a Sabbath-rest is not merely relief from labor but participation in God's own completed-work rest, the seventh-day satisfaction of Genesis 2. Ellicott emphasizes the positive and permanent character of what remains: since Joshua's Canaan-rest did not exhaust the promise, and since the promise of God cannot fail, a Sabbath-rest — sharing in God's own Sabbath — remains as a standing reality for the people of God. Barnes observes that the people of God is the community defined by faith: those who believe are God's people, and for them this rest is not a vague future but a certain inheritance. Clarke finds the word sabbatismos wonderfully comprehensive: it encompasses the rest of faith already enjoyed in this life (the peace of trusting God), the rest of death (the soul's release from toil), and the final Sabbath of the resurrection (complete, eternal rest in God's presence). Wesley applies it immediately: the rest that remains is not only future — it begins now, in the soul that trusts God's word and ceases from its anxious self-reliance. Robertson notes that sabbatismos is a hapax legomenon, formed on the analogy of other words meaning a keeping or celebrating of the Sabbath — the rest is rest-in-the-manner-of-God's-Sabbath.

voices.html: A Sabbath-rest remains — the word sabbatismos is chosen deliberately to echo the creation Sabbath of Genesis 2. The rest that remains is not merely the cessation of activity but participation in the divine rest itself: the satisfaction of completed work, the joy of the Creator who saw that it was good and rested. This is the inheritance of the people of God — to share in God's own Sabbath.

The word sabbatismos appears only here in the New Testament, apparently coined by the author. It means a keeping of the Sabbath, a Sabbath-rest — not just any rest but rest in the mode of the divine Sabbath. Since Joshua's rest did not exhaust the promise, this Sabbath-rest remains outstanding. The people of God are those who will enter it by faith.

There remaineth — the word apoleipetai used in verse 6 recurs here. Something remains, is still outstanding, has not been claimed. The Sabbath-rest of God, prefigured by the seventh day of creation and by the Canaan promise, remains for the people of God. It is not exhausted, not cancelled, not limited to a single generation. It waits for all who come to God through faith.

Sabbatismos — a rest of the Sabbath-kind. This is the full rest of the people of God: begun in this life through faith and the peace it brings, continued in death when the soul rests from its labors, and completed in the resurrection when body and soul together enjoy the eternal Sabbath of God's presence. All three phases are included in the word that remains for the people of God.

10.synthesis: For he that is entered into his rest, he also hath ceased from his own works, as God did from his. The pronoun he is the exegetical crux. Calvin, Ellicott, and Clarke apply it to the believer who enters God's rest and, in so doing, ceases from his own works — the anxious, merit-seeking self-effort that is the opposite of faith. Just as God ceased from his works at creation's completion, so the believer ceases from the works of self-justification and enters the rest of trusting God's completed work in Christ. Barnes follows this reading: the believer's entry into rest is characterized by the cessation of self-reliance, just as God's Sabbath was characterized by the completion and cessation of creative work. Wesley sees in this a profound picture of sanctification: as faith grows, the soul increasingly rests in God rather than in its own exertions. However, some have read the pronoun as referring to Christ: Christ who has done his work of redemption entered the divine rest, and believers enter rest through him. The author's immediate application to the readers in verse 11 — let us labour to enter into that rest — suggests the pronoun is applied to believers. Ellicott acknowledges the possible Christological reading but favors the believer-application as the more coherent reading in context. Clarke resolves the tension by noting that both readings are true theologically, but the grammatical antecedent is most naturally the believer.

voices.html: He who has entered into God's rest ceases from his own works as God ceased from his. The works here are not good works or obedience, but the works of self-justification — the anxious effort to earn standing before God by one's own merit. Faith ends that effort, just as creation's completion ended God's creative labor. This is the rest of faith: ceasing from the impossible task and trusting in what Christ has completed.

The pronoun most naturally refers to the believer in context. The one who enters God's rest ceases from his own works in the same way God ceased from his at creation. The analogy is precise: God's rest was the rest of a finished work; the believer's rest is the rest of trusting a finished work — the redemption accomplished by Christ — rather than attempting to construct his own righteousness.

As God ceased from his works on the seventh day — the works being complete — so the believer who enters God's rest ceases from the works of self-justification. The rest is not idleness but the peace of one who has stopped trying to earn what has been freely given. Faith receives; law strives. The Sabbath-rest is the rest of reception rather than effort.

Some apply the pronoun to Christ, who completed his redemptive work and entered the divine rest. Others apply it to the believer. Theologically both are true: Christ entered his rest by completing his work, and the believer enters rest by trusting that completed work. The grammatical antecedent in context is the believer of verse 9, but the believer's rest is grounded entirely in Christ's prior entry into his.

10.key_tension: The pronoun in verse 10 is most naturally applied to the believer (Calvin, Ellicott, Clarke, Barnes) who ceases from self-justifying works upon entering God's rest, but a Christological reading — Christ who completed redemption and entered his rest — is theologically coherent and noted by several commentators.

11.synthesis: Let us labour therefore to enter into that rest, lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief. The exhortation contains a famous paradox: let us labour to enter into rest. Calvin appreciates the paradox rather than dissolving it: the labor is real — sustained, earnest exertion of faith against the constant pull of unbelief — yet what is labored for is rest. The Greek spoudāsōmen means to strive earnestly, to be zealous. Ellicott notes that there is no contradiction: entering rest requires the vigorous exercise of faith, just as a runner who aims at stillness must run hard to reach the finishing line. Barnes observes that the warning lest any man fall after the same example of unbelief returns directly to the wilderness generation: their example is the standing cautionary tale. To fall after that example is to repeat their pattern — hearing the word, having access to the promise, and failing through the same unbelief. Clarke stresses that the example is named of unbelief (apeitheia), not of outward sin alone: the root cause that the readers must avoid is the internal disposition of mistrust, not merely the external behaviors it produces. Wesley makes the labor concrete: the exertion required is the daily, active exercise of faith against doubt, the consistent orientation of the heart toward God's promise rather than toward the apparent security of turning back. Robertson notes the aorist subjunctive spoudāsōmen — a decisive, urgent call to strenuous effort in the present moment.

voices.html: Let us strive to enter into that rest — the paradox is intentional. We must work hard to obtain rest. The labor is the earnest exercise of faith against every temptation to let go of the promise. The rest is not obtained without effort, not because it must be earned, but because faith itself requires sustained, vigilant activity against the drift of the human heart toward unbelief.

There is no real contradiction in laboring to enter rest. The athlete labors toward the finish line; the finish line is not labor but completion. So the believer strives in faith — vigorously, earnestly — toward the rest that awaits. The warning lest any fall after the same example of disobedience keeps the wilderness generation as the perpetual cautionary image throughout the argument.

The example of unbelief — the wilderness generation — is the pattern to avoid. Their fall was not sudden; it was the accumulation of small refusals to trust God, each hardening the heart until the final verdict was just. The striving the author commends is the consistent, active exercise of faith that guards against that accumulation. No one falls all at once; unbelief grows in silence until it is too late.

Let us be diligent — the word implies earnest, wholehearted effort. This is the spirit with which faith must be exercised: not a casual assent to doctrinal propositions, but an active, daily turning of the heart toward God's promise. The same example of unbelief that destroyed the wilderness generation waits to destroy any who approach the gospel with the same half-heartedness.

12.synthesis: For the word of God is quick, and powerful, and sharper than any twoedged sword, piercing even to the dividing asunder of soul and spirit, and of the joints and marrow, and is a discerner of the thoughts and intents of the heart. Having spoken of the striving required, the author now grounds it in the character of the word that both promises rest and warns against falling short. Calvin finds here a reminder that the word of God is not passive material for human assessment; it assesses the reader. Ellicott notes the key terms: quick (zōn) means living — the same word used of the living God in 3:12; powerful (energēs) means active, operative. Barnes is struck by the sword imagery: a two-edged sword cuts on both sides — the word of God both encourages the faithful and judges the hardened. Clarke observes the anatomical precision of the penetrating metaphor: soul and spirit, joints and marrow — the word reaches the innermost depths of human nature, the places where no human eye can penetrate. Wesley draws the personal application: no one can hide from this word, not even behind a correct external performance. The word discerns thoughts and intents — the hidden motivations beneath the visible behavior. Robertson notes that the word for discerner (kritikos) is the root of our word critic: the word of God is the true critic, the judge, of what is actually happening within the human heart. The verbal form zōn echoes the earlier reference to the living God (3:12), suggesting that the word shares in the life and authority of the God who speaks it.

voices.html: The word of God is living and active — it does not merely lie before the reader waiting to be assessed; it assesses the reader. No one can approach Scripture as a neutral observer and remain untouched. The word is sharper than any sword not because it inflicts pain but because it reaches deeper than any physical instrument: to the soul, the spirit, the hidden motivations of the heart that no human eye can see.

Living — the same Greek word (zōn) used of the living God in chapter 3. The word shares in the life of the God who utters it; it is not a dead record but a living voice. Active (energēs) means operative, effective — it accomplishes what it says. The two-edged sword cuts both ways: it both saves and judges, both comforts and convicts, depending on the heart that receives it.

Sharper than any two-edged sword — a sword with two edges cuts on both sides; the word of God cuts in every direction. Soul and spirit, joints and marrow: the pairing moves from the psychological to the physical, suggesting that no realm of human nature is exempt from the word's penetration. The word that promises rest also judges the heart that refuses to enter.

The word kritikos (discerner) is the root of our word critic. The word of God is the supreme critic — the only one qualified to judge the thoughts and intents of the heart. Enthumēseōn (thoughts) and ennoiōn (intents) are paired as the contents of the inner life that no external observer can access. The word reaches where human introspection cannot.

13.synthesis: Neither is there any creature that is not manifest in his sight: but all things are naked and opened unto the eyes of him with whom we have to do. The vivid word opened translates the Greek tetrachēlismenā, a term whose precise meaning was debated even in antiquity but which most commentators render as the image of an animal with its neck bent back for slaughter, completely exposed. Calvin notes that the verse moves from the character of the word (v. 12) to the character of the God who speaks it: nothing is hidden from him. The practical logic is clear: since the word of God reaches the innermost recesses of the heart (v. 12), and since God sees every creature with perfect clarity (v. 13), there is no corner of the soul in which the warning of chapters 3–4 can be evaded. Ellicott observes the shift from the word to the divine speaker: him with whom we have to do is God himself, the one to whom every human being is ultimately accountable. Barnes finds the application sobering: no degree of external conformity conceals the inner condition from God. The reader who maintains a Christian profession while harboring the evil heart of unbelief (3:12) is not deceiving God. Clarke notes the judicial language: creatures are laid bare before the divine tribunal. Wesley stresses that this is not only a warning but an encouragement to honesty in prayer: since everything is known, there is no value in concealment and no barrier to approaching God as one truly is. Robertson notes that tetrachēlismenā may also carry the image of wrestling — the neck exposed in a throw — but the core sense is total exposure before a superior.

voices.html: All things are naked and opened before him. The movement from the word to the God who speaks it is deliberate: the word penetrates (v. 12); God sees completely (v. 13). There is nowhere to hide. The person who maintains an outward Christian performance while cultivating an inner heart of unbelief is not deceiving God but only himself — and the self-deception is precisely what the word is given to expose.

The word tetrachēlismenā — the term for a creature with its neck bent back, fully exposed for inspection or slaughter — conveys the absolute vulnerability of all creatures before God's sight. Nothing is concealed; the innermost thoughts revealed by the word (v. 12) are also seen by the God before whom every creature stands fully known and fully accountable.

Naked and opened to the eyes of him with whom we have to do — the phrase with whom we have to do names the relationship: God is not a distant observer but the one to whom we are personally and permanently accountable. The totality of divine knowledge — of every thought, intent, and hidden motive — is the foundation of both the warning and the invitation to come to him honestly.

The image of all things naked and exposed before God is judicial: we stand before a tribunal from which nothing is hidden. This is the final ground of the warning about hardening: the one who hardens his heart against God's voice does not do so unobserved. Every evasion, every rationalization, every quiet accommodation to unbelief is seen by the one who discerns the thoughts and intents of the heart.

14.synthesis: Seeing then that we have a great high priest, that is passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold fast our profession. After the searching warnings of verses 12–13, the author pivots to the great positive ground of assurance: the church has a high priest who has passed through the heavens. Calvin notes that the phrase passed through the heavens distinguishes the Christian high priest from the Aaronic: Aaron entered the earthly Holy of Holies once a year and withdrew; Christ has passed through the heavens themselves and entered the presence of God permanently. Ellicott observes that the aorist dielhyluthota (having passed through) indicates a completed action whose effects remain — Christ is now, permanently, at the right hand of the Father as our high priest. Barnes stresses the name: Jesus the Son of God — the human name and the divine title are held together, ensuring that the high priest who sympathizes with our weakness (v. 15) is the same as the Son who has all authority. Clarke finds in passed through the heavens a reference to the ascension as the completion of Christ's high-priestly work: the offering was made on earth; the presentation of the offering was made in heaven. Wesley draws the practical exhortation from the theology: the reason to hold fast the profession is not willpower but the greatness of the priest who intercedes. Robertson's Word Pictures notes that dielhyluthota is a perfect participle — the completed action endures; Christ remains in heaven as our priest, not returning periodically as Aaron did.

voices.html: We have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens. The contrast with Aaron is deliberate: Aaron went through the veil into the earthly Holy of Holies and came out again; Christ has passed through the heavens themselves and remains there, permanently, as our priest. His high-priestly ministry is not intermittent but continuous. Let us therefore hold fast — we have a priest who does not abandon his office.

The perfect participle dielhyluthota — having passed through — expresses a completed action whose effects endure. Christ is not presently passing through the heavens; he has done so, and now permanently occupies the divine presence as our high priest. The exhortation to hold fast our profession rests on this permanent priestly reality: the one who intercedes for us is always there.

Jesus the Son of God — the human name and the divine title in combination. He who sympathizes with our weakness as the man Jesus is also the Son of God with all authority and power. His high priesthood unites perfect sympathy with perfect power. To hold fast our profession is to hold fast to the one who holds fast to us.

Passed through the heavens — not merely into the first heaven (the atmosphere) or the second (the starry sky) but through all intermediate spheres into the very presence of the Father. This surpasses Aaron's annual entry into the earthly sanctuary by an infinite distance. Our high priest is not behind a veil that he must pass through annually; he is in the eternal Holy of Holies, permanently, on our behalf.

15.synthesis: For we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities; but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin. This verse is among the most personally comforting in the letter. The construction is a double negative: we do not have a high priest who cannot sympathize — meaning we do have one who can and does. Calvin observes that the author is heading off a potential pastoral objection: if Christ is so exalted, so transcendent, having passed through the heavens, how can he understand the struggles of ordinary believers? The answer is his genuine incarnation and genuine temptation. Ellicott stresses that in all points means the entire range of human temptation — not every individual temptation arithmetically, but every class of human temptation. The one exception is crucial: without sin (chōris hamartias). Clarke notes that this exception does not diminish the sympathy but completes the qualification: Christ entered every human temptation without yielding, which means he knows the full weight of temptation more than any who have yielded, for those who yield discover only what temptation offers at the surface, while the one who endures feels its full force. Barnes finds deep comfort in the word touched (sympathēsai — to sympathize): it implies not intellectual awareness of human weakness but genuine emotional resonance with it, the feeling of it alongside us. Wesley stresses that the sympathy is active, not passive: Christ is touched by our infirmities now, at the right hand of the Father, as our interceding high priest. Robertson notes that chōris hamartias is an adverbial phrase modifying the temptation, not the person: he was tempted apart from sin — without it being the occasion of his sinning.

voices.html: We have a high priest who can be touched with the feeling of our infirmities. The construction says first what he is not — not unable to sympathize — in order to insist that he is exactly the opposite: genuinely sympathetic, genuinely able to feel alongside us. He was tempted in all things as we are, yet without sin. The sinlessness does not reduce the sympathy; it purifies it, keeping it from the distortions that sin introduces into human compassion.

Tempted in all points like as we are — not every individual temptation but every category of human temptation. Yet without sin: this qualification does not make the sympathy less real but more intense. The person who has resisted temptation to the end knows its full pressure; the person who yields feels only the beginning of it. Christ's sinlessness means he bore the full weight of every temptation without relief.

Touched with the feeling of our infirmities — the word sympathēsai means to suffer alongside, to feel what another feels, not merely to know about it. Christ does not observe our weaknesses from a distance; he has felt them himself. This is the ground of his sympathy: not merely that he knows about our trials, but that he has experienced the weight of them in his own person.

In all points tempted as we are, yet without sin. He was tempted in the wilderness, in Gethsemane, throughout his ministry — hunger, weariness, grief, opposition, desertion. Every kind of human trial came to him. Yet he sinned not. This is our comfort: the one who intercedes for us knows from the inside every temptation we face, and has already overcome every one of them.

16.synthesis: Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need. The chapter — and with it the extended argument from chapter 3 — closes on an invitation. The word boldly (parrēsian, the same word used in 3:6) appears here as the characteristic posture of the believer before God: not trembling approach, not the terror of one who has been warned, but open, confident access to the divine throne. Calvin insists on both words: boldly and throne of grace. The throne that might have been a throne of judgment has been made a throne of grace by the priestly work of Christ. Ellicott observes that come (proserchōmetha) echoes the Levitical language of approach to the divine presence — the very approach that the old covenant surrounded with elaborate preparation and restriction. In Christ, the restriction is lifted; the approach is open. Barnes finds the two purposes of the approach precisely matched to the two offices of chapter 4: we obtain mercy for past failure (the language of forgiveness) and find grace to help in time of need for present and future trial (the language of ongoing provision). Clarke is moved by in time of need — not after the crisis has passed, not in retrospect, but eukairos, at the well-timed moment of need. Wesley draws the pastoral conclusion that closes the chapter's argument: having been warned against the evil heart of unbelief, and having been shown the great high priest who sympathizes and intercedes, the reader is left not with fear but with an open invitation: come boldly, now, and receive what the wilderness generation forfeited by refusing to trust the God who offered it.

voices.html: Let us come with boldness to the throne of grace. The throne is grace — it has been constituted as a throne of grace by the priestly work of Christ. We do not approach a judge in whose hands our fate is uncertain; we approach a throne that exists to give mercy and provide help. The boldness commended is not arrogance but the confidence of those who know the character of the one they approach.

The approach to the throne of grace echoes the old covenant's approach to the divine presence — but everything has changed. What required the elaborate mediation of the Aaronic priesthood, with its annual restrictions and the terror of the Holy of Holies, is now open to every believer through Christ. Come boldly is not hyperbole; it is the new-covenant reality made possible by the great high priest who has passed through the heavens.

Mercy for past failures; grace to help in time of need — the two gifts precisely match what believers require. Mercy covers what has already gone wrong; grace provides what is needed for what lies ahead. Both are available, freely, at a throne that exists to give them. The invitation is complete: come boldly, come now, and receive both.

In time of need — the Greek is eukairos, well-timed, seasonable. Grace is not given in advance of the need, stored up for eventual use; it comes at the moment of need, neither too early nor too late. This is the final pastoral word: whatever the trial, whatever the temptation to harden the heart, the throne of grace is there — and the one seated on it is the very high priest who has felt what we feel and overcome what we face.

1.synthesis: Hebrews 7 opens with a formal introduction of Melchizedek, drawn from the brief Genesis 14 narrative: king of Salem, priest of the Most High God, who met Abraham returning from battle and blessed him. Calvin observes that the author has been preparing his readers throughout the preceding chapters for exactly this comparison, and now enters fully into what he had before only sketched. Ellicott notes the grammatical structure: the sentence begun here is not completed until verse 3, where Melchizedek is said to "abide a priest continually" — the connection to chapter 6's closing promise of a forerunner-priest is therefore deliberate and tight. RWP calls these opening verses "a little sermon" on 6:20, noting that the author follows rabbinic interpretive method but adapts it well for Jewish readers. Barnes explains that Abraham's tithing of the spoils was "not so much designed as a present to Melchizedek personally, as an act of pious thankfulness to God" — he recognized Melchizedek as a genuine minister of God. The verse establishes the typological premise the whole chapter will argue: one who is simultaneously king and priest, and who stands above even Abraham, is the fitting type of Christ.

voices.html: He now enters fully into what he had before slightly referred to; for he enumerates particularly the things connected with Melchisedec, in which he resembled Christ. It is indeed no wonder that he dwells so minutely on this subject. It was doubtless no common thing that the Holy Spirit should recommend this person in so remarkable a manner.

The sentence is completed in the last words of Hebrews 7:3 — "abideth a priest continually"; the connection with the last chapter, therefore, is very clear. Of Melchizedek we know nothing beyond what we learn from the brief narrative of Genesis 14.

It is a daring thing to put Melchizedek above Aaron, but the author does it. Moffatt calls verses 1–3 "a little sermon" on 6:20. It is "for ever" that he explains. Melchizedek is the only one in his line and stands alone in the record in Genesis.

This does not appear to have been, on the part of Abraham, so much designed as a present to Melchizedek personally, as an act of pious thankfulness to God. He doubtless recognised in Melchizedek one who was a minister of God, and to him devoted the tenth of all which he had taken, as a proper acknowledgment of the goodness of God.

2.synthesis: The verse draws out the double significance of Melchizedek's name and title. "King of righteousness" is the literal meaning of his Hebrew name; "King of Salem" means King of Peace. Both Josephus and Philo, as Ellicott notes, recognized the theological weight of these names, and the author deploys them typologically: the Messiah unites righteousness and peace in his own person. Wesley captures this precisely: "in him, as in Christ, righteousness and peace were joined" — an echo of Psalm 85:10. The tithes Abraham gave were, as Barnes explains, an act of worship directed through Melchizedek toward God rather than a personal gift to the man. Clarke observes that the ancient practice of consecrating a tenth of battle-spoils to the gods was widespread; Abraham's gesture acknowledged God's hand in the victory and Melchizedek's role as the legitimate mediator of blessing. The verse thus enriches the typology: Melchizedek is not merely a historical figure but a symbolic image whose very name anticipates Christ's dual office as the righteous king who makes peace.

voices.html: King of righteousness. Josephus notes the significance of this name: "The first founder of Jerusalem was a chief of the Canaanites, who in our tongue is called Righteous King; for indeed such he was." Philo also interprets King of Salem as "King of Peace." The special interest of these titles for the writer lies in the application to Jesus the Messiah.

Being first — According to the meaning of his own name. King of righteousness, then — According to the name of his city. King of peace — So in him, as in Christ, righteousness and peace were joined. And so they are in all that believe in him.

It was an ancient custom, among all the nations of the earth, to consecrate a part or tenth of the spoils taken in war to the objects of their worship. This however was not according to any provision in law, but merely ad libitum, and as a eucharistic offering to those to whom they imagined they owed the victory.

Abraham's tithe does not appear to have been designed as a present to Melchizedek personally, as much as an act of pious thankfulness to God. He recognised in Melchizedek one who was a minister of God, and devoted the tenth of all which he had taken as a proper acknowledgment of the goodness of God and of his claims.

3.synthesis: "Without father, without mother, without descent, having neither beginning of days, nor end of life" — this is the most theologically charged description of Melchizedek in Scripture, and commentators differ meaningfully on how much weight to give its silences. Ellicott and Robertson both insist the argument rests on what Genesis does not say: Melchizedek appears as priest without any genealogical credentials, unlike every Levitical priest whose legitimacy depended on traceable descent. In this way the narrative itself presents him as standing by sheer personal right rather than by hereditary appointment. Calvin, however, presses deeper into the typological dimension: the silence about Melchizedek's birth and death is not merely a literary observation but a deliberate prefiguring of Christ's eternal priesthood. The type exempts Melchizedek from "what is common to others, a descent by birth; by which he means that he is eternal." Calvin is careful to add, however, that this does not mean the historical man himself still lives. Clarke raises the theological difficulty that Christ — unlike Melchizedek — does have a Father in heaven and a mother on earth, but argues the type holds at the level of priestly office: neither priesthood depends on genealogical succession. Wesley states the devotional reading most simply: Melchizedek is "in all these respects... made like the Son of God."

voices.html: The Apostle immediately explains his meaning by adding "without kindred." He then exempts Melchisedec from what is common to others, a descent by birth; by which he means that he is eternal, so that his beginning from men is not to be sought. We are not hence to conclude that the man who met Abraham is yet alive, as some have childishly thought.

Not because we find no mention of the parents of Melchizedek is he thus spoken of as fatherless and motherless, but because he is suddenly introduced as priest, without any token whatever that he held the office by right of genealogy, the only claim familiar to Hebrew readers.

The argument is that from silence, made much of by Philo, but not to be pressed. The record in Genesis tells nothing of any genealogy. Melchizedek stands alone. He is not to be understood as a miraculous being without birth or death. Melchizedek has been made more mysterious than he is by reading into this interpretation what is not there.

Without father, without mother, without pedigree — Recorded, without any account of his descent from any ancestors of the priestly order. But being — In all these respects. Made like the Son of God — Who is really without father, as to his human nature; without mother, as to his divine; and remaineth a priest continually.

3.key_tension: Ellicott and Robertson read the silence on Melchizedek's origins as a genealogical argument from absence — he simply lacks the credentials Levitical priests required; Calvin reads the same silence as a deliberate typological representation of Christ's genuinely eternal, non-hereditary priesthood.

4.synthesis: "Now consider how great this man was" — the imperative calls readers to deliberate attention. The measure of Melchizedek's greatness is the fact that Abraham the patriarch paid him tithes. Barnes explains the rhetorical logic: Jewish readers held Abraham in the deepest veneration; if Melchizedek stood above Abraham, then whoever stood above Melchizedek stood above all Abraham's descendants, including every Levitical priest. RWP notes the Greek word πηλίκος (how great) implies geometrical rather than arithmetical magnitude — a quality of distinction rather than a quantity. Calvin observes that the author is deliberately selective: of all the reasons tithes might be given, he focuses only on the one that reveals rank and honor. Ellicott notes that "patriarch" (from the LXX) denotes the supreme head of a clan — Abraham was not merely an ancestor but the pre-eminent figure of the entire nation, and even he showed deference. The verse thus serves as the launching point for the extended argument of vv. 4–10: Abraham's act was not incidental but revelatory of an acknowledged hierarchy that placed Melchizedek above the entire Israelite priestly lineage before that lineage even existed.

voices.html: Though tithes were instituted for several reasons, yet the Apostle here refers only to what serves his present purpose. The more glorious Abraham is made, the higher the dignity of Melchisedec appears. For this purpose he says that Abraham had the promises — by which he means that he was the first of the holy race with whom God made the covenant.

The greatness abides, set forth in the words of Scripture. "Patriarch" is a word used in the LXX for the head of a family or chief of a clan. In the New Testament it is applied to Abraham, David, and the sons of Jacob — the supreme ancestors of the nation.

The Jews had a profound veneration for Abraham; and if it could be shown that Melchizedek was superior to Abraham, then it would be easy to demonstrate the superiority of Christ, as a priest, to all who descended from Abraham. He to whom even the patriarch Abraham showed so much respect must have had an exalted rank.

How great (πηλικος) — Geometrical magnitude in contrast to arithmetical (ποσος), here only in N.T., meaning "how distinguished." He received tithes from Abraham and blessed Abraham, and even Levi is included in the argument.

5.synthesis: The verse establishes a baseline: Levitical priests had legal authority to collect tithes from their fellow Israelites — all of whom, like the Levites themselves, descended from Abraham. This legal right, however real, was bounded in two ways: it derived from external law rather than personal authority, and it operated only within the family of Abraham. Wesley arranges the hierarchy with precision: the people stand below the Levites, the Levites below the priests, and the priests below Abraham — who himself stands below Melchizedek. Ellicott clarifies a surface difficulty: technically the Levites (not the priests) received the primary tithe, but the supreme authority to exact tithes resided in the priestly office as guardians of all religious observance. Clarke adds a further distinction: the priests received a tenth of the Levites' tenth — a tithe within the tithe — which illustrates that even within the Levitical system there were gradations of precedence. But all these internal distinctions are subsidiary to the main point: that the entire hierarchy, from the humblest Israelite to the high priest himself, traces back to Abraham, and Abraham paid tithes to someone who stood entirely outside this family structure.

voices.html: The sons of Levi take tithes of their brethren — Sprung from Abraham as well as themselves. The Levites therefore are greater than they; but the priests are greater than the Levites, the patriarch Abraham than the priests, and Melchisedec than him.

The authority to exact tithes was in strictness vested in the priests, the supreme guardians of the laws relating to all religious duties and observances. The question is not one of emolument, but of position.

The Levites received a tenth from the people. The priests received a tenth of this tenth from the Levites, who are here called their brethren, because they were of the same tribe, and employed in the same sacred work. The apostle is proceeding to show that Melchisedec was greater even than Abraham himself.

The Levitical priests had a right to receive tithes of their brethren, but still that they were inferior to Melchizedek. The illustrious ancestor of the whole Jewish people — Abraham — had paid tithes to one who belonged to no Israelite tribe at all.

6.synthesis: Verse 6 presents two actions that together demonstrate Melchizedek's superior standing: he received tithes from Abraham, and he blessed him. The first had already been introduced; the second deepens the argument. Ellicott draws out what makes Melchizedek's superiority distinctive: it is not legal (he holds no office within any Israelite system) but inherent — he appears in Genesis as one whose authority is simply acknowledged. Calvin calls this the fifth comparison between Christ and Melchizedek, noting the principle the verse assumes: "the inferior is blessed by his superior." Clarke reads the blessing typologically: Melchizedek was "the representative of the Messiah, the promised seed," so in blessing Abraham he prefigured the way all nations would ultimately receive blessing through Christ. Barnes focuses on the priestly nature of the act: it is not a simple wish for good but a formal declaration of God's intention toward another, made by one who "sustains a higher relation to God than ordinary men." The phrase "him that had the promises" is significant — it elevates Abraham's dignity even as it shows his subordination, making Melchizedek's superiority all the more remarkable.

voices.html: This is the fifth comparison between Christ and Melchisedec. The Apostle assumes it as an admitted principle that the less is blessed by the greater; and then he adds that Melchisedec blessed Abraham: hence the conclusion is that the less was Abraham. For the more glorious Abraham is made, the higher the dignity of Melchisedec appears.

In Melchizedek we see a man who, though no law gave him pre-eminence, takes tithes of Abraham, and therefore appears in Scripture as holding a position of inherent and acknowledged superiority. This superiority is now presented still more strikingly in connection with the blessing.

As in the seed of Abraham all the nations of the earth were to be blessed, Abraham received a sacerdotal blessing from Melchisedec, who was the representative of the Messiah, the promised seed, to show that it was through him, as the high priest of the human race, that this blessing was to be derived on all mankind.

7.synthesis: The verse offers an axiom: it is always the lesser who is blessed by the greater. The proposition is stated as self-evident — "without all contradiction" — and all voices treat it as such, directing their energy toward what the principle means when applied to Melchizedek's blessing of Abraham. Calvin carefully distinguishes this priestly act of blessing from ordinary mutual well-wishing: it is a formal, public act by which one who holds high public office "recommends to God men in private stations and under his ministry." Clarke, following Macknight, explains the same point: the blessing is "the action of a person authorized to declare God's intention to bestow good things on another" — like Isaac's blessing of his sons or a prophet's blessing of the people, it carries divine authorization, not merely human goodwill. Barnes connects this to the broader principle: when a minister pronounces blessing on a congregation, the act presupposes the minister stands in a higher relation to God than those beneath his care. RWP notes the Greek idiom: the neuter construction ("the less... the greater") is a standard Greek generalizing form. All voices apply the axiom without dispute: Melchizedek's blessing of Abraham establishes, without any further argument, that Melchizedek was Abraham's superior.

voices.html: The word "bless" means here a solemn praying by which he who is invested with some high and public honor recommends to God men in private stations and under his ministry. Another way of blessing is when we pray for one another; which is indeed mutual, and belongs to all alike; but the blessing here spoken of is that of superiors only.

The blessing here spoken of is not the simple wishing of good to others, which may be done by inferiors to superiors; but it is the action of a person authorized to declare God's intention to bestow good things on another. In this manner Isaac and Jacob blessed their children under a prophetic impulse; in this manner the priests under the law blessed the people.

The act of pronouncing a blessing is understood to imply superiority of rank, age, or station. When a minister of religion pronounces a blessing on a congregation, the same thing is understood — it is the act of one who sustains a higher relation to God than ordinary men, and who is appointed to declare his will.

8.synthesis: The verse draws a spatial contrast — "here" and "there" — between the Levitical economy and the Genesis narrative. Here, tithes are received by dying men; there, by one of whom Scripture witnesses only that he lives. Under the Levitical order, the priests who received tithes were mortal: their authority began with birth and ended with death, making them subject to the same fundamental limitation as those they served. Ellicott makes the decisive point: Melchizedek's authority in Genesis rests entirely on "the mere fact of his life" — it is personal, not institutional; it does not derive from a system that continues after he does. Calvin reads this typologically with appropriate care: the silence about Melchizedek's death functions as evidence of his life — not meaning the historical man still walks the earth, but that as a type, what his Scripture-record represents (unending priesthood) belongs fully to Christ, in whom the type finds its reality. Wesley states the contrast simply: Melchizedek is "not spoken of as one that died for another to succeed him; but is represented only as living, no mention being made either of his birth or death." The argument from silence is deliberate, not accidental: the author reads Scripture's narrative silences as theologically significant.

voices.html: He takes the silence respecting his death as an evidence of his life. This would not indeed hold as to others, but as to Melchisedec it ought rightly to be so regarded, inasmuch as he was a type of Christ. We are not hence to conclude that the man who met Abraham is yet alive, as some have childishly thought, for this would be absurd.

Here, under the Levitical economy, dying men receive the various tithes. There, in the history now considered, one receives tithes of whom the Scripture simply witnesses that he lives. The narrative of Genesis gives no other basis for his priesthood than the mere fact of his life. What he holds, he holds by personal right.

And here — In the Levitical priesthood. But there — In the case of Melchisedec. He of whom it is testified that he liveth — Who is not spoken of as one that died for another to succeed him; but is represented only as living, no mention being made either of his birth or death.

Under the Jewish dispensation, though the priests were successively removed by death, yet they were as duly replaced by others appointed from the same family. But there is no account of Melchisedec ceasing to be a priest, or of his dying; he is represented as still living, the better to point him out as a type of Christ.

9.synthesis: The argument reaches its boldest point: even Levi himself, who in his descendants receives tithes, can be said to have paid tithes to Melchizedek — through Abraham. The phrase "as I may so say" (ὡς ἔπος εἰπεῖν) openly signals that the author is aware this is a figurative argument, not a literal historical claim. Clarke's philological survey shows this idiom was standard in the best classical Greek writers — Aristotle, Philo, Josephus, Demosthenes — used precisely to soften a startling proposition while preserving its substance. Wesley states the logic plainly: "not in person, but in his successors, as it were, paid tithes — in the person of Abraham." RWP is frank that this is "a rabbinical imaginative refinement," but notes the author deploys it with rhetorical skill aimed at readers steeped in representative reasoning. The underlying principle is one Jewish theology accepted: the acts of an ancestor can be ascribed to descendants because they were, in a seminal sense, present. If this is so, then Levi — the tithe-receiving tribe — stands in the position of tithe-payer before Melchizedek, and therefore before Christ. Calvin addresses the potential objection about Judah (ancestor of Christ) also being in Abraham's loins and therefore also having "paid tithes": the logic applies specifically to priestly dignity, not universally.

voices.html: He advances farther, and says, that even Levi himself, who was then in the loins of Abraham, was not exempt from the same subordination; for Abraham, by paying tithes, made himself and his posterity inferior to the priesthood of Melchisedec. Even Levi, who was then in the loins of his father when Melchisedec met him, paid tithes in Abraham.

Και ὡς επος ειπειν — And so to speak a word. This form of speech, very frequent among the purest Greek writers, is generally used to soften some harsh expression, or to limit the meaning when the proposition might otherwise appear to be too general. It answers fully to our "so to speak" — as one would say — I had almost said.
And even Levi, who received tithes — Not in person, but in his successors, as it were, paid tithes — In the person of Abraham.

So to say (ως επος ειπειν) — An old idiom, here only in the N.T., common in Philo, used to limit a startling statement. Hath paid tithes (δεδεκατωτα) — Perfect passive indicative of δεκατοω, "has been tithed." This could only be true of Levi "so to speak." This is a rabbinical imaginative refinement appealing to Jews.

10.synthesis: Verse 10 grounds the preceding argument in a brief, plain statement: Levi was in the loins of Abraham — not yet born, not yet conceived as an individual — when Abraham met Melchizedek. This is the explanatory premise of the "as I may so say" in verse 9. Clarke states it tersely: "Levi was seminally included in Abraham, his forefather." Barnes elaborates the ancient principle of representative inclusion: the relationship between Abraham and his future descendants was such that "the ancestor's acts can be ascribed to the descendants" because they were genuinely present in him, even if not yet individually realized. RWP keeps the philological note concise: Abraham is here called Levi's "father" (πατρός) in the extended sense of forefather, which is common usage. The argument is admittedly a rabbinical mode of reasoning — it operates within the framework of solidarity between ancestor and descendant that Jewish theology took for granted — but within that framework it is logically tight. If the descendant shares the ancestor's identity in principle, the ancestor's acts of homage carry forward. Levi's subsequent receiving of tithes, therefore, does not reverse this original posture of deference; it operates within a system that already stood below Melchizedek before Levi existed.

voices.html: For he was yet in the loins of his father — That is, Levi was seminally included in Abraham, his forefather.

Abraham is here called the father of Levi, by a common use of the word, referring to a more remote ancestor. The relation which subsisted between him and Abraham implied the same thing as if he had then been born, and had acted for himself by paying tithes. Instances of this occur constantly in the Old Testament.
In the loins of his father (εν τη οσφυ του πατρος). Levi was not yet born. The reference is to Abraham, the forefather (πατρος) of Levi. This is a rabbinical imaginative refinement appealing to Jews.

11.synthesis: Verse 11 is the pivot of the chapter, shifting from the historical argument about Melchizedek and Abraham to the logical argument for a change of priesthood. The pivot is a simple conditional: if the Levitical priesthood had achieved its purpose — if it had produced "perfection" — then no new priest would have been necessary. But Psalm 110:4 promises one. The very existence of the promise is the indictment. Clarke defines τελείωσις precisely: not merely improvement, but "the completing or finishing of any thing, so as to leave nothing imperfect, and nothing wanting" — specifically, giving the people a fully adequate standing before God. Ellicott draws the implication: the Melchizedekian order is "altogether different from and higher than the order of Aaron," and this could only be necessary if the Aaronic order had failed at its central task. Calvin extends the logic: priesthood and law were structurally bound together under Moses; when the priesthood was declared inadequate, the law governing it was implicitly declared inadequate too. Barnes notes the resistance this argument would have faced: Jewish readers "were accustomed to regard the system as perfect." RWP, quoting Moffatt, names the defect plainly: the Levitical priesthood "failed to give men a perfectly adequate relation to God."

voices.html: From the same testimony the Apostle concludes that the old covenant was abrogated by the coming of Christ. As God had instituted a priesthood for the purpose of ratifying the Law, the former being abolished, the latter must also necessarily be abolished. He has hitherto spoken of the office and person of the priest.

The order of Melchizedek is altogether different from, and higher than, the order of Aaron. This being so, how could this other priesthood take the place of the Levitical if this latter had answered its full purpose? Perfection — literally, the making perfect — the full accomplishment of the essential aim of priesthood, in bringing men near to God.

The word τελειωσις signifies the completing or finishing of any thing, so as to leave nothing imperfect, and nothing wanting. Applied here to the Levitical priesthood, it signifies the accomplishment of that for which a priesthood is established: giving the Deity an acceptable service, pardoning all offenses, purging the conscience from guilt, and purifying the soul.

The condition is of the second class, "if there were perfection, etc." The Levitical priesthood failed to give men "a perfectly adequate relation to God" (Moffatt). The adjective Levitical occurs in Philo.

12.synthesis: The change in priesthood logically entails a change in law, since the two were structurally inseparable under Moses. Calvin presses this to its fullest implication: Christ becomes not only priest but lawgiver, absorbing the roles of both Aaron and Moses into his single office — "the right of Aaron, as well as of Moses, was transferred to him." Barnes enters a crucial qualification that all readers need: the "law" undergoing change here is the ceremonial, priestly law — not the moral law. The ten commandments cannot be abrogated because they "grow out of the nature of things" and are universal and perpetual; but the regulations governing sacrifice, purification, priestly descent, and temple ceremony were entirely dependent on the Levitical priesthood and therefore fall with it. Ellicott states the point economically: "If the priesthood is changed there takes place also of necessity a change of law." Clarke observes that the very essence of the Levitical system was its sacrificial offerings; without those, the system has no substance. RWP frames the result typologically: God's appointment of a priest from outside Aaron's order "left the Levitical line off to one side, forever discounted, passed by 'the order of Aaron.'"

voices.html: As the authority of the Law and the priesthood is the same, Christ became not only a priest, but also a Lawgiver; so that the right of Aaron, as well as of Moses, was transferred to him. The sum of the whole is, that the ministry of Moses was no less temporary than that of Aaron; and hence both were annulled by the coming of Christ.
If the priesthood is changed there takes place also of necessity a change of law. It is no light matter to speak of the order of Aaron as set aside: this carries with it a change of law.

The law so far as it grew out of that, or was dependent on it. This could not apply to the ten commandments — for they were given before the institution of the priesthood; nor could it apply to any other part of the moral law, for that was not dependent on the appointment of the Levitical priesthood.

God's choice of another kind of priesthood for his Son left the Levitical line off to one side, forever discounted, passed by "the order of Aaron" (την ταξιν Ααρων). A change (μεταθεσις) — old substantive from μετατιθημι, in N.T. only in Hebrews.

13.synthesis: The argument moves from the prophetic to the historical. The "other priest" of Psalm 110:4 is Jesus, and Jesus is from the tribe of Judah — a tribe from which no one had ever legally ministered at the altar of Israel. The priestly law was specific: priesthood belonged to Levi, and to Levi alone. Clarke states the consequence plainly: a man from Judah "could not minister according to the law." This is not a concession but a proof: if Christ is a priest — and his followers confessed he was — then the law governing priesthood has already changed, in fulfillment of Psalm 110. Calvin notes that the author draws on the readers' own admission: those addressed confessed Jesus to be the Christ, and that confession is precisely what makes the argument irresistible — they cannot dispute his priesthood without denying their own faith. Ellicott draws out the Greek: the word for "pertaineth" (μετεσχηκεν) carries a sense of genuine participation — the same word used in 2:14 where Christ "took part" in human flesh. He is genuinely, fully of Judah. Wesley summarizes the double shift: the priesthood has passed from one order (Aaron) and from one tribe (Levi) to another order and another tribe entirely.

voices.html: The Apostle was speaking to them who confessed Jesus to be the Christ; and their own admission is used to prove the argument. As the Apostle was speaking to them who confessed Jesus to be the Messiah, he takes this as a settled principle and proves the change of priesthood from the tribe of Christ himself.

Jesus the Messiah, spoken of in Psa 110:4, who came, not from the tribe of Levi, but from the tribe of Judah, of which tribe no priest ever ministered at a Jewish altar, nor could minister according to the law.

But the priesthood is manifestly changed from one order to another, and from one tribe to another. For he of whom these things are spoken — Namely, Jesus. Pertaineth to another tribe — That of Judah. Of which no man was suffered by the law to attend on, or minister at, the altar.
Pertaineth — literally, hath partaken of: the same word is used in Hebrews 2:14, "He also took part of the same." A different tribe, from which no man hath given attendance at the altar.

14.synthesis: Jesus' descent from Judah was, at the time of writing, publicly established and historically beyond dispute. Clarke makes the apologetic observation: if genuine genealogical doubt had existed, opponents of the gospel would have pressed it as a decisive refutation; their silence confirms the genealogical record was clear. Barnes notes a further dimension: the Messiah's descent from Judah was itself an Old Testament expectation (Genesis 49:10, Isaiah 11:1), so the fulfillment carries prophetic weight beyond mere historical fact. Ellicott draws attention to the Greek verb for "sprang" (ἀνατέταλκεν — perfect active of ἀνατέλλω, "to rise up"): in every other New Testament use, this word describes the rising of the sun, the morning star, or the dawn. The prophetic imagery of Numbers 24:17 and Malachi 4:2 uses the same root for the Messiah. Ellicott therefore sees in the author's word choice a deliberate echo of the "Branch" prophecies: Christ is the one who has arisen from Judah as a new light. The verse closes the factual premise and opens the deeper argument of verse 15: not only does he come from the wrong tribe for a Levitical priest, but he belongs to an entirely different priestly order.

voices.html: Sprang — Better, hath arisen out of Judah. In every other place in the New Testament this word is applied to the rising of the sun, the light, the day-star, or the clouds; and in the prophecies of Numbers 24:17 and Malachi 4:2 the same word is used. The word also denotes the springing up of plants, and occurs in the LXX in the Messianic prophecy of "the Branch."

As the apostle speaks here with so much confidence, it follows that our Lord's descent from the tribe of Judah was incontrovertible. Whatever difficulties we may find in the genealogical tables now, there were none apprehended in those days, else the enemies of the Gospel would have urged these as a chief and unanswerable argument against Christ.

It was a doctrine of the Old Testament, and was admitted by the Jews, that the Messiah was to be of the tribe of Judah. This was an additional consideration to show that there was to be a change of some kind in the office of the priesthood, since it was declared that the Messiah should be of a tribe to which the priesthood did not pertain.
For it is evident that our Lord sprang out of Judah — Whatever difficulties have arisen since, during so long a tract of time, it was then clear beyond dispute.

15.synthesis: The verse escalates the force of the argument: from "evident" (v14) to "yet far more evident." RWP notes the pile of intensifying words in the Greek (περισσότερον ἔτι καταδηλον — "still more abundantly thoroughly clear") as rhetoric stacking emphasis on emphasis. Wesley identifies the double shift that makes this proof stronger than the preceding one: not only does the new priest come from a different tribe (Judah instead of Levi), but he belongs to a different order entirely (Melchizedek rather than Aaron). These are two independent proofs, each conclusive on its own; together they are overwhelming. Calvin identifies the deeper distinction at stake: the Levitical order was constituted by external, ceremonial rites — what he calls "carnal" in v16 — while the Melchizedekian order is spiritual, a difference not of lineage but of substance. Clarke anchors the whole argument in Psalm 110:4, where God himself swore an oath naming this change: the very fact that God made an oath signals that what follows is irrevocable. Barnes adds that the strangeness of the promise — a priest from outside the priestly tribe, belonging to an older and higher order — is precisely what makes it so decisive against any claim that the Levitical system was permanent or sufficient.

voices.html: He proves by another argument that the Law is abolished. He reasoned before as to the person of the priest, but now as to the nature of the priesthood. The ancient priesthood had to do with external rites; but in Christ's priesthood there is nothing but what is spiritual. It hence appears that the former was evanescent and temporary; but that the latter was to be perpetual.

It is more abundantly strikingly manifest that God designed the Levitical priesthood to be changed, because of the oath in Psa 110:1–7, where, addressing the Messiah, he says: Thou art a Priest for ever after the order, or similitude, of Melchisedec, who was not only a priest, but also a king. None of the Levitical priests sustained this double office.
And it is still far more evident, that — Both the priesthood and the law are changed, because the priest now raised up is not only of another tribe, but of a quite different order.

Yet more abundantly evident (περισσοτερον ετι καταδηλον) — Only N.T. instance of the old compound adjective, thoroughly clear, with ετι (still) added and the comparative περισσοτερον (more abundantly), piling Ossa on Pelion. After the similitude (ομοιοτητα) — See 4:15 for this word.

16.synthesis: The nature of the two priesthoods is now stated at its deepest level. The Levitical priesthood rested on a "carnal commandment" — a law of flesh. The Melchizedekian priesthood rests on "the power of an endless life." Clarke carefully distinguishes what "carnal" means here: not sinful or corrupt, but "feeble, frail, what may be said of man in his present dying condition." The Levitical law was tied to human descent, human ceremonies, and human mortality — all legitimate but all inherently perishable. RWP captures the Greek precisely: σαρκινής ("fleshen") means constituted of flesh, not merely pertaining to it; and ζωῆς ἀκαταλύτου means "indissoluble life" — a life that cannot be dissolved or destroyed. Ellicott emphasizes that the carnal commandment was "limited to the sphere of man's nature of flesh" — bound by distinctions of race, tribe, family, and death. Wesley identifies the source of the endless life: Christ holds it "in himself, as the eternal Son of God" — not as a gift received from outside, but as an intrinsic property of his divine nature. Calvin connects the point to the earlier argument: the external character of the old priesthood foreshadowed its temporariness; the spiritual character of the new priesthood foreshadows its perpetuity. The contrast is ontological at its root.

voices.html: It was called carnal because it refers to things corporal, that is, to external rites. What was fulfilled in Christ by the hidden and celestial power of the Spirit was shadowed forth under the Law by ointment, various vestments, the sprinkling of blood, and other earthly ceremonies. This kind of institution was suitable to the nature of the priesthood; it hence follows that the priesthood itself was liable to change.

A commandment of flesh: one that is limited to the sphere of man's nature of flesh. As such, it is bound up with distinctions of race and tribe and family; it is limited by human infirmity and the changes wrought by sickness and death; what it accomplishes is the purifying of the flesh; in its own nature it is temporary, and may be set aside.

Carnal does not always mean sinful or corrupt, but feeble, frail, or what may be said of or concerning man in his present dying condition. But after the power of an endless life — Not dying, or ceasing through weakness to be a priest, but ever living, and ever energetic.

Carnal (σαρκινης) — "Fleshen" as in 1Co 3:1, not σαρκικης (fleshlike). The Levitical priests became so merely by birth. Of an endless life (ζωης ακαταλυτου) — Late compound, indissoluble. Jesus as priest lives on forever. He is Life.

17.synthesis: Psalm 110:4 is quoted once more, now explicitly as divine testimony to the "power of an endless life" just declared. Calvin locates the entire argumentative weight on one word: "for ever" (εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα). This single adverb distinguishes the Melchizedekian order from the Aaronic at the most decisive point: mortality cannot be "for ever." If the priest is priest forever, he cannot die; if he cannot die, he was not appointed under the carnal commandment. Clarke draws out the logical chain plainly: "if he be a priest for ever, he ever lives; if he ever lives, he ever acts as a priest; if he ever acts as a priest, he ever makes intercession for sinners." Barnes frames the same point as divine confirmation: "this is proved by the testimony of God himself, that he was to be a Priest for ever." Ellicott, following what he regards as the better manuscript reading, prefers "witness is borne to him" — making Scripture's testimony to Christ's priestly character the subject of the sentence rather than a simple quotation formula. RWP notes that the author "aptly quotes again" Psalm 110:4, returning to the anchor text as the epistle's argument mounts.

voices.html: It is on the single word "for ever" that the Apostle lays stress in this passage; for he confirms what he said of an endless life. He then shows that Christ differs from the whole race of Levi, because he is made a priest for ever.

This is the proof that he was not appointed according to the carnal commandment, but according to the power of an endless life, because he is a priest for ever; i.e. one that never dies, and is never disabled from performing the important functions of his office; for if he be a priest for ever, he ever lives.

That this is the true account of it is proved by the testimony of God himself, that he was to be a Priest for ever. The quotation is from Psalm 110:4, which the apostle uses throughout this argument as referring to the Messiah.

18.synthesis: The Levitical law is not merely superseded but annulled — the Greek ἀθέτησις is a legal term that RWP notes was common in the papyri to mean "making void." Two grounds are given: weakness and unprofitableness. Clarke gives the sharpest exposition of weakness: the law "had no energy; it communicated none; it had no Spirit to minister; it required perfect obedience, but furnished no assistance to those who were under it." Wesley states the double failure concisely: "insufficiency either to justify or to sanctify." Barnes draws the necessary boundary: this annulment applies to the ceremonial, priestly law — not the moral law. The moral law, growing from the unchangeable nature of God and of human moral obligation, "cannot be abrogated." Calvin frames the law's weakness without denigrating it: the ceremonies had no intrinsic saving power, but the promise of grace "annexed to them" did not belong to the ceremonies themselves — it belonged to the sacrifice they typified. The law was thus weak not because it was poorly designed but because it was always a vessel pointing beyond itself, and once what it pointed to had come, the vessel was no longer needed. Ellicott notes that this "disannulling" was implicit already in verse 12's word "change" (μετάθεσις).

voices.html: He explains the reason why it ought to have been abolished, even because it was weak and unprofitable. He speaks thus in reference to the ceremonies, which had nothing substantial in them, nor in themselves anything available to salvation; for the promise of favor annexed to them did not properly belong to sacrifices, but to Christ alone, who was typified by them.

It had no energy; it communicated none; it had no Spirit to minister; it required perfect obedience, but furnished no assistance to those who were under it. And unprofitableness — No man was benefited by the mere observance of its precepts: it pardoned no sin, changed no heart, reformed no life.

There is implied in this new and everlasting priesthood, and in the new dispensation connected therewith, a disannulling of the preceding commandment — An abrogation of the Mosaic law. For the weakness and unprofitableness thereof — For its insufficiency either to justify or to sanctify.

A setting aside. The law which existed before in regard to the priesthood becomes now abrogated. This does not refer to the moral law, as if that were abrogated, for that law cannot be abrogated. It grows out of the nature of things, and must be perpetual and universal.

19.synthesis: The verdict on the Levitical law is total: it "made nothing perfect."
