12.synthesis: The typological parallel is now made explicit: as the bodies of the sin-offerings were burned outside the camp, so Jesus suffered outside the gate of Jerusalem, in order to sanctify the people through his own blood. Ellicott traces both sides of the parallel with precision: the Levitical regulation (Leviticus 16; Numbers 19) required the sin-offering to be consumed outside the camp; and Jesus was crucified outside the walls of Jerusalem, at the place called Golgotha. This was not coincidence but fulfillment — he who in all other points fulfilled the law of atonement fulfilled it also in this. Clarke develops the theological purpose: "that he might sanctify the people" — not merely as priest performing a ritual but as the reality to which all the rituals pointed, consecrating them to God and making atonement for their sins by his own blood, not the blood of animals. Barnes stresses the voluntariness: it is implied that Jesus chose this place and this manner of death in conformity with the type. RWP draws the structural connection to the earlier argument of chapters 9–10: the parallel with the great Day of Atonement ritual is here brought to its climactic and most concrete expression.

voices.html: The sin-offering was burned without the camp. Jesus, who in all other points fulfilled the law of atonement, fulfilled it in this point also, in that he suffered "without the gate" (Matthew 27:32; John 19:20). The two words — "without the camp" and "without the gate" — are the hinge on which the practical exhortation of the next verse turns.

That he might sanctify the people — that he might consecrate them to God, and make an atonement for their sins, he suffered without the gate at Jerusalem, as the sin-offering was consumed without the camp when the tabernacle was in the wilderness. The parallel is exact: place, purpose, and means all correspond to the type — and all surpass it, for the blood is his own.

Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood. There might be a conformity between his death for sin and the sacrifices which typified it. It is implied that it was voluntary — he chose to be led outside the city, to Golgotha, in fulfillment of the type. And by that death, and by that blood, he accomplished what the types could only signify: actual sanctification.

13.synthesis: The typological argument drives a practical conclusion: let us then go forth to him outside the camp, bearing his reproach. The move from ritual to moral exhortation is characteristic of the epistle's method, and Calvin notes that what might otherwise be a frigid allegorical exercise is here given life by the practical duty it requires. To "go forth outside the camp" means to leave the entire system of Judaism — its temple, its priesthood, its social honor — and to join Christ in the position of one rejected and cast out. Ellicott makes the social point sharply: Jesus suffered outside the gate as a sign of rejection by his own people; to follow him means accepting that rejection as one's own. Clarke reads it with an eye to the imminent destruction of Jerusalem: leave this city and this system, devoted to destruction, and take refuge in Jesus alone. Barnes draws out the full emotional weight: Christ was put to death as a malefactor, an object of contempt and scorn; to go forth to him means willingness to share that social position — the reproach of being identified with one whom the world crucified. Wesley is brief: go forth without the camp, out of the Jewish dispensation, bearing his reproach — all manner of shame, obloquy, and contempt for his sake.

voices.html: That the preceding allegory might not be frigid and lifeless, he connects with it an important duty required of all Christians. The figure of going outside the camp is not left as a puzzle: it demands that we follow Christ to the place of rejection, that we bear his reproach, that we are willing to lose the social honor of being insiders in the religious establishment in order to be with him who was cast out.

The suffering "without the gate" was a symbol of Christ's rejection by the Jews. All who would be his must share the reproach which came upon him — cast out by his people and crucified. They also must go outside — outside the securities and the social acceptability of the old religion — to where he is. To remain inside the camp is to remain with those who rejected him.

Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp — as if going forth with him when he was led away to be crucified. He was put to death as a malefactor, an object of contempt and scorn. To follow him means willingness to share that contempt — to be identified with one who was cast outside the walls of religious respectability. His reproach is the reproach we bear.

14.synthesis: The call to go forth outside the camp is grounded in a theological reality: we have no continuing city here. The earthly Jerusalem, however venerable, is not the ultimate home; Christians live as pilgrims oriented toward the city to come. Calvin sees the verse extending the concept of going forth: not only are we to leave the Jewish system, but we are to recognize that our whole earthly existence is a journey, not an arrival. Ellicott finds a double thought: we are free to leave the earthly city because we are not bound to it — our hopes rest with no abiding earthly sanctuary — and we are willing to bear reproach because the reproach belongs to our condition as pilgrims seeking what is not yet visible. Barnes connects the verse back to chapter 11's portrait of the patriarchs, who sojourned in tents without possessing the promised land because they looked for a city whose builder and maker is God (Hebrews 11:10). RWP observes, with historical irony, that the epistle must have been written before the destruction of Jerusalem — if after, the argument here would have been clinched by reference to the actual fall of the city. Wesley notes simply: "All things here are but for a moment; and Jerusalem itself was just then on the point of being destroyed."

voices.html: He extends still further the going forth which he had mentioned, even that as strangers and wanderers in this world we should consider that we have no fixed residence but in heaven. Whenever therefore we are driven from one place to another, are exiles, or sojourn in a foreign land, let us raise our minds to that celestial country where our citizenship truly is.

In this verse there seems to be a union of two thoughts: we are free to go forth from the city so long held sacred, for our hopes are bound up with no abiding earthly sanctuary; and we may not shrink from the reproach attached to that going forth, for we are already seekers of a city still to come, and pilgrims cannot be choosy about their resting places.

For here we have no continuing city. We do not regard this as our final home or our fixed abode, and we should be willing to bear reproaches during the little time we are to remain here. The patriarchs confessed themselves strangers and pilgrims on the earth (Hebrews 11:13), looking for a heavenly country; Christians stand in the same posture, bearing the reproach of Christ while pressing toward the city to come.

15.synthesis: Having argued for the abrogation of the Levitical sacrifice system (chapters 7–10), the author now specifies what sacrifice remains and is expected: the sacrifice of praise, the fruit of lips confessing his name. Calvin notes this is the author's way of answering the objection that, if the ceremonial sacrifices are abolished, Christians have nothing to offer God; his answer is that praise and thanksgiving are the appointed sacrifices of the new age, and that these are offered not in our own right but through Christ as our high priest. Ellicott stresses "through him" (di' autou): it is only through Christ's mediation that even our praise is acceptable to God; he is the altar (verse 10) and the priest through whom our priestly offering ascends. Barnes expands the Old Testament background: Psalm 50:14, 23; Hosea 14:2 — the prophets anticipated that the truly acceptable offering would be the fruit of lips, not the blood of bulls. Wesley keeps the theological economy tight: the two sacrifices praised here — praise and beneficence — are the two pillars of Christian worship, corresponding to love of God and love of neighbor. RWP notes the present imperative "let us keep on offering" — this is not a once-for-all act but a continuous and ceaseless practice.

voices.html: He returns to that particular doctrine respecting the abrogation of ancient ceremonies and anticipates an objection: if sacrifices are abolished, what remains for us to offer? His answer: the sacrifice of praise — giving thanks to his name — offered through Christ our high priest. This is the sacrifice of the new covenant, and it is by no means a lesser offering; it is more than what the temple ever received, for it is the homage of hearts redeemed and renewed.

By him — through him. Through his sacrifice, which has made atonement, we are hallowed and fitted for our priestly service (1 Peter 2:5). Let us offer up a sacrifice of praise to God continually — the sacrifice that replaces the Levitical altar is continuous, not periodic; it is the fruit of lips giving thanks to his name, the voice of those who have been brought near to God by Christ's blood.

By him therefore — the Jews approached God by the blood of the sacrifice and the ministry of their high priest. We approach through Christ. The exhortation is founded on the general argument of the epistle: since we have such a high priest, let us use him — let us offer the sacrifice of praise which he makes acceptable. The fruit of lips which acknowledge his name is the new covenant's appointed worship.

16.synthesis: The sacrifice of praise (verse 15) is complemented by the sacrifice of beneficence: doing good and sharing (communicating) with others. Calvin notes that acts of love and mercy are themselves sacrifices acceptable to God, and that those who imagine the only acceptable worship is ceremonial have entirely missed this. The verse ties Christian charity directly to worship: giving to the needy is not a secular good deed but a liturgical act — an offering on the altar of the neighbor. Ellicott draws the connection explicitly: with thankfulness to God must be joined acts of well-doing to men, both being presented as sacrifices. Barnes observes that "to communicate" (koinōnia) means sharing what one has with others, living in a community of goods and care rather than private accumulation. Clarke connects this to the context: the readers were being called to leave behind social security (the community of the synagogue) and bear reproach; acts of mutual generosity would sustain the community that was bearing that cost together. RWP notes the genitive construction and the vocabulary: eupoiia (doing good) appears only here in the New Testament, while koinōnia (communication, sharing) is the rich fellowship-word of the Pauline letters applied here to material sharing.

voices.html: Here he points out another way of offering a due and regular sacrifice: all the acts and duties of love are so many sacrifices. He thereby intimates that those were foolish who thought that by abolishing the Mosaic rites, the whole of worship was taken away. No — the sacrifice of doing good and sharing with others remains, and God is well-pleased with these offerings more than with the blood of bulls and goats.

And yet another offering may we bring: with thankfulness to God must be joined acts of well-doing to men; these too being presented as sacrifices to God. To communicate — freely to impart to others. See Romans 12:13. The two sacrifices of this verse and the preceding answer to the two great commandments: love of God expressed in praise, love of neighbor expressed in sharing.

But to do good and to communicate, forget not. To communicate or impart to others — that is, to share with them what we have. The meaning is that they were not to be forgetful of the duty of doing good to others and of contributing to their needs. With such sacrifices God is well pleased — they are not inferior to the praise of the lips but its proper complement, the outward fruit of the inward gratitude.

17.synthesis: The exhortation to obey present leaders (contrasting with the retrospective remembrance of verse 7) grounds the duty in the accountability of pastors before God. Calvin takes "those who have the rule over you" as referring specifically to spiritual leaders in the church, not civil magistrates, and finds the grounds of obedience in the spiritual relationship: they watch over souls as those who will give account. Ellicott notes the framing: the chapter opens and closes (verses 7 and 17) with references to church leaders, creating a bracket around the intervening practical duties. The verb "watch" (agrypnousin) suggests sleeplessness — an active, costly vigilance — and Barnes draws out the weight this puts on the pastor: one who watches through the night bears a burden that calls forth the congregation's active support, not passive resistance. Wesley captures the qualified submission: give up not your conscience or your judgment, but your own will, in all things purely indifferent — obedience to spiritual authority stops at the boundary where it would require sin. Clarke reminds readers that "to rule" implies also the capacity to guide: the same word carries both authority and the ability to lead well. The motivation offered is both personal (that it may be done with joy, not grief) and communal (grief in a pastor is unprofitable to those under his care).

voices.html: I doubt not but that he speaks of pastors and other rulers of the Church, for there were then no Christian magistrates; and what follows properly belongs to spiritual government. He commands first obedience and then submission. The ground of both is that they watch for souls as those who must give account: a pastor who must answer to God for each soul is not a figure to be resisted lightly.

The present section of the chapter begins (verse 7) and ends (verse 17) with a reference to the rulers of the Church: remember your former leaders, and imitate their faith; obey those who lead you now. They watch over souls as those who will render an account — the vigil is costly and the accountability real. Let their watching be done with joy rather than with groaning, for that is to the readers' own advantage.

Obey them that have the rule over you — the reference is to religious teachers, not civil rulers. They are to show proper respect and submit to their authority in matters of religion. The grounds given are practical: the leaders watch for souls as men who must give account; their work becomes joy or grief depending on the cooperation they receive; and grief in a pastor is not without unprofitable consequences for his people.

18.synthesis: The author requests prayer for himself and his companions, grounding the request in a declaration of good conscience — a conscience that bears no guilt and seeks to live honorably in all things. Calvin observes that even apostolic success depended in some measure on the prayers of the church; the request is an acknowledgment of the interdependence of minister and congregation. Barnes cites Paul's similar self-commendations (1 Thessalonians 5:25; Ephesians 6:18–19) and notes that to solicit prayer while asserting a good conscience is not self-congratulation but a guarantee of sincerity: the author has no hidden agenda that prayer would expose. Ellicott notes the personal tone that begins with verse 18 and continues through verse 23 — personal notices about the writer's situation and acquaintances. RWP detects in the phrase "behaving honestly" (kalōs) an awareness that unworthy motives have been attributed to the author — the same defensiveness Paul shows in 1 Thessalonians 2:18 and 2 Corinthians 1:17. Clarke observes that the prayer of a congregation for its minister is not a luxury but a bounden duty, and that few Christian communities feel it as they ought. The appeal to a good conscience is both a personal assurance and a pastoral invitation: pray for us because we have no reason to be ashamed of what your prayers will help accomplish.

voices.html: After having commended himself to their prayers, in order to excite them to pray, he declares that he has a good conscience. Though indeed our prayers ought to embrace the whole world, as love does; yet it is right and proper to pray especially for those who minister to our spiritual needs. And here the minister's declaration of good conscience removes any obstacle that might prevent fervent intercession.

Pray for us — even the success of apostles depended, in a certain way, on the prayers of the church. Few Christian congregations feel, as they ought, that it is their bounden duty to pray for the success of the gospel by the mouths of those who minister to them. The declaration of a good conscience both grounds and invites that prayer: we ask you to intercede for us who have nothing to hide from God or from you.

Pray for us. This is a request which the apostle often makes in his own behalf and in behalf of his fellow-labourers. For we trust we have a good conscience — the request for prayer rests on the author's conviction of sincerity. It is not a boast but an assurance: what we are asking you to pray for is a work we pursue with unclouded conscience, in honourable reliance on God.

19.synthesis: The author adds a more personal and urgent dimension to the prayer request: he asks especially that he might be restored to his readers sooner through their intercessions. Calvin reads this as an additional incentive to prayer: the prayers of the congregation are not merely for the author's benefit in the abstract but for their own, since his presence among them would profit them all. Ellicott infers from this verse that the writer had formerly been associated with these readers and had been separated from them by some constraint — whether imprisonment, enforced travel, or other circumstances cannot be determined. Barnes confirms that the verse clearly implies an adverse circumstance preventing the visit: something has kept the writer from coming, and prayer is appealed to as the means of removing it. RWP notes the construction of the purpose clause — "that I may be restored to you the sooner" — using apokatastathō, a verb used of restoring someone to their former place (Matthew 12:13; Acts 1:6), which may suggest a return after separation. Clarke observes the affectionate pastoral spirit: the writer does not think primarily of his own comfort or safety but of the benefit his presence would bring to the readers and the work of God among them.

voices.html: He now adds another argument: the prayers they make for him will be profitable to them all as well as to himself individually. As though he had said: "I do not so much consult my own benefit as the benefit of us all; for if through your prayers I am the sooner restored to you, we shall mutually enjoy the fruit of it together." Prayer for the minister is therefore prayer for the whole congregation.

And I exhort you the more abundantly to do this — all that we can certainly infer from this verse is that the writer had formerly been associated with these readers and was now separated from them. He hopes to be restored to them sooner by their prayers — the urgency of the more abundantly implies that something concrete is at stake, some adverse circumstance that prayer might move.

That I may be restored to you the sooner. It is here clearly implied that the writer was deterred from visiting them by some adverse circumstances over which he had no control — possibly imprisonment, possibly the duties of distant ministry. The prayer requested is for the removal of whatever obstacle stands between the writer and the congregation whose welfare is the underlying concern.

20.synthesis: The closing prayer-benediction is one of the richest in the New Testament, packing the epistle's chief theological themes into a single sentence. Calvin notes that the author prays for what he has been exhorting throughout, recognizing that human exhortation alone cannot produce what only God can work. "The God of peace" — Ellicott traces this Pauline phrase (Romans 15:33; 16:20; 1 Thessalonians 5:23) and notes that its use here follows, as almost always, a context of strife or discord. The resurrection of Christ is named as the act of "bringing back from the dead" the shepherd of the sheep — the great shepherd — by virtue of the blood of the eternal covenant; Calvin and Barnes both observe that this is the only direct reference to the resurrection in Hebrews, and it comes at the end as the capstone of everything: the high priest who offered himself once for all was raised by God, whose resurrection validates the sacrifice. The covenant sealed by that blood is called eternal — contrasting with the temporary Mosaic covenant throughout the epistle — and the prayer that flows from these realities is that God himself would equip the readers for every good work, working within them what is pleasing to him through Jesus Christ.

voices.html: To render mutual what he had desired them to do, he ends his Epistle with prayer. He asks of God to perfect them; such is the meaning of katartisai. We hence conclude that we are by no means fitted for our duty until God forms and perfects us. And he derives his confidence in praying from the resurrection of Christ, which is the pledge that God accepts his sacrifice and fulfills his covenant.

The God of peace — in almost all cases where this Pauline phrase occurs there is something in the context suggestive of strife or disorder. The resurrection of Christ is here introduced as the ground of confidence: the God who brought back from the dead that great shepherd of the sheep by the blood of the eternal covenant is well able to equip his people for every good work that flows from that same covenant.

God who is the author and source of peace. The word peace in the New Testament denotes every kind of blessing or happiness. Who brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus — this is the only direct reference to the resurrection in this epistle, and it is placed at the climax: the risen shepherd lives to intercede; the eternal covenant stands validated; and the prayer that follows is grounded on all of this.

21.synthesis: The prayer request from verse 20 is now articulated: that God would make the readers fully ready (katartisai) in every good work to do his will, working in them what is pleasing in his sight through Jesus Christ. Calvin's comment on the word is exact: katartizō means to put in full order, to fit, to equip — the image is of a body set in proper joint (compare 2 Corinthians 13:9). The prayer acknowledges both the divine initiative and the human response: God works within, but the result is actual human action in every good work. Ellicott distinguishes the two Greek words for making perfect used in the epistle (one associated with completion of state, the other with equipping for function), and identifies the one here as the equipping verb — the prayer is for practical fitness for service, not merely for moral status. Barnes clarifies the scope of "every good work": not a general competence but the specific works that constitute a pleasing life before God — those described in the practical exhortations of this chapter. RWP notes the optative mood (katartisai) — a wish or prayer for the future — and the paradox of "working in us" while we do the good works: divine agency and human activity are not rivals but partners in the moral life. The doxology rounds the prayer with the traditional formula of praise to God through Jesus Christ forever.

voices.html: He gives a definition of good works by laying down God's will as the rule: no works are to be deemed good but such as are agreeable to the will of God. And he shows that the source of good works is God's grace, not human nature — hence the prayer that God would make them perfect, fitting them for every good work. The glory is to be rendered to God through Jesus Christ, in whom alone all our good works have worth.

Make you perfect (katartisai) — to equip for service, not merely to complete in state. The prayer is for practical fitness: that every good work listed in this chapter — hospitality, compassion for prisoners, honoring marriage, contentment, following leaders, doing good and sharing — might be wrought in them by God himself, through the mediation of Jesus Christ.

Make you perfect — the word means to make fully ready, to put in full order; to make completely adequate for a purpose. The apostle here does not affirm that they were then perfect but prays for that full fitness for service which would make their performance of every good work complete. Working in us that which is well pleasing — the divine activity is interior; the resulting good works are exterior; both are required for the fully equipped believer.

22.synthesis: The author closes with a gentle apology and an appeal for reception. He asks the readers to "bear with the word of exhortation" — to receive the exhortation of the epistle with patience — and justifies the request by noting that he has written briefly. Both Calvin and Ellicott are struck by the modesty of the description "briefly," given the length of the letter: Calvin suggests the author is acknowledging that he has written less than the subject deserved; Ellicott notes that in comparison with what might have been said about the infinite resources of the new covenant, the epistle is indeed compressed. Barnes reads the phrase "word of exhortation" as an appropriate title for the whole epistle, which is indeed a sustained practical appeal to persevere in faith. Clarke observes that the epistle, by showing the insufficiency of the Levitical system and the all-sufficiency of Christ, has made a comprehensive case; the brevity is relative to the magnitude of the subject. Wesley is practical: the letter is longer than the author's usual correspondence, yet it is still brief given the copiousness of the theme. The appeal to bear with it acknowledges that some of the argument has been demanding, and that patient reading is itself an act of faith.

voices.html: Some understand this as though he was soliciting them to hear him; but I think he mentions that he has written briefly in order not to appear as though he wished to lay too heavy a burden on them. He modestly says "briefly" — not because the epistle is short, but because the subject is so vast that no treatment of it, however long, could be considered adequate.

But I exhort you, brethren, bear with the word of exhortation; for indeed it is in few words that I have written unto you. How fitly the whole Epistle may be spoken of as an "exhortation" has already appeared. "In few words" — in relation to the inexhaustible richness of the subject, the treatment is indeed brief. The appeal to "bear with" it is an acknowledgment that patient reception is required.

Suffer the word of exhortation — referring to the arguments and counsels in this whole epistle, which is in fact a practical exhortation to perseverance in adhering to the Christian religion amidst all the temptations to apostatize. I have written a letter unto you in few words — in a brief compass, considering the greatness of the subject and the importance of the truths discussed.

23.synthesis: The brief personal notice about Timothy — "know that our brother Timothy has been set at liberty" — is one of the most discussed verses in the epistle for what it implies about authorship. Calvin notes that the Greek allows either "know ye" (imperative: informing them of something new) or "ye know" (indicative: assuming shared awareness), and prefers the imperative, treating this as news being communicated. Ellicott observes that the readers knew of Timothy's imprisonment but had not yet heard of his release — the information is being passed on. Barnes notes that this verse and its Pauline associations have been used as evidence for Pauline authorship, though the question cannot be settled from this verse alone; he documents its importance in the discussion. RWP treats the verse as evidence that the writer was a companion of Paul and Timothy, was aware of their situations, and expected the readers to know who Timothy was — consistent with a Pauline circle of authorship. Clarke cites manuscript evidence for "our brother" and uses it to argue for a close personal relationship between the writer and Timothy. The planned joint visit — "with whom, if he comes soon, I will see you" — is a warm pastoral touch that gives the epistle a personal, relational close.

voices.html: Since the termination of the Greek verb will admit of either reading, we may read "ye know" or "know ye." I prefer the latter, though I do not reject the other. The apostle had apparently received news of Timothy's release and is communicating it to readers who knew of the imprisonment but not yet of the liberation. The intention to visit with Timothy adds a concrete, personal close to the letter.

It is clear that the Hebrew Christians knew of the imprisonment of Timothy but had not heard of his release. In 2 Corinthians 1:1; Colossians 1:1; Philemon 1:1, Timothy is spoken of as "the brother"; in 1 Timothy and 2 Timothy he is the recipient of instruction. The news of his liberty, and the hope of a joint visit, tie this letter into the network of the Pauline mission circle.

Know ye that our brother Timothy is set at liberty. On the meaning of this and its importance in determining who was the author of the epistle, the notes on the introduction should be consulted. Whatever the authorship, the verse provides a historical datum: Timothy had been in some form of confinement and was now free, and a joint visit to the readers was contemplated.

24.synthesis: Salutations to the leaders and to all the saints — both the local teachers (compare verse 7's specific group) and the whole congregation are included in the greeting. Calvin suggests that saluting the rulers specifically is a mark of honor designed to encourage them in their difficult calling. The final clause — "those from Italy greet you" — has generated sustained discussion about the epistle's place of composition. Ellicott presents both hypotheses with characteristic balance: either the writer is in Italy and sends greetings from Italian believers to the readers elsewhere, or the writer is outside Italy and people originally from Italy who are with him send greetings homeward. Barnes does not settle the question. RWP simply notes the ambiguity: "Those with the author in Italy, or those who have come from Italy to the author outside of Italy" — both remain possible. Clarke's reading of "all the saints" as the general Christian community confirms that the salutation is intended to be as broad as possible, reaching every member of the congregation without exception. The double salutation — to the leaders separately and to all together — models the inclusive pastoral care the epistle has been calling for.

voices.html: As he writes his Epistle generally to the Hebrews, it is natural that he bids some separate from the rest to be saluted; but he sends this salutation more particularly to the rulers, as a mark of honor, that by this distinction he might show that he had a special esteem for them, and would thus encourage them in their difficult and weighty charge.

These much discussed words are consistent with either of two hypotheses: that the writer is in Italy sending greetings outward, or that those who have come from Italy to the writer's present location are sending greetings back to their home congregation. Neither reading can be definitively excluded on internal evidence alone, and the question is inseparable from the larger question of authorship and destination.

Salute all them that have the rule over you. Hebrews 13:7, 17. None are mentioned by name — the salutation goes to the leaders as a class and to all the saints broadly. They of Italy salute you — the phrase is among the most debated in the epistle, equally consistent with the writer being in Italy or abroad with Italian companions. It remains an unresolved datum in the question of provenance.

24.key_tension: "They of Italy greet you" is consistent with either the writer in Italy sending greetings outward, or Italian believers abroad greeting their home congregation; Ellicott and Barnes both acknowledge the ambiguity cannot be resolved from internal evidence.

25.synthesis: "Grace be with you all" — the epistle closes with the briefest possible benediction, the same formula found at the close of the Pastoral Epistles (Titus 3:15; 1 Timothy 6:21; 2 Timothy 4:22) and Colossians 4:18. Ellicott notes the formulaic character and the kinship with the Pauline letter closings; the "all" is inclusive — every member of the congregation, however divided or wavering, is embraced in the final prayer of grace. Barnes observes that the subscriptions attached to the epistle in some manuscripts ("written to the Hebrews from Italy by Timothy") carry no manuscript authority and should not be used as evidence for any claim about the epistle's composition. Clarke's comment on "grace" captures the theological weight: the divine favor — that free and undeserved gift that has been the epistle's subject from beginning to end, displayed supremely in the blood of the eternal covenant (verse 20) — is what the author desires to rest upon and remain with every reader. The single word charis (grace) is in a sense the entire epistle compressed: grace revealed in the Son, grace mediated by the great high priest, grace received by faith, grace that sustains in suffering. The epistle both begins and ends in grace.

voices.html: Grace be with you all. This brief closing benediction is also found in Titus 3:15 and, with the omission of "all," in Colossians 4:18; 1 Timothy 6:21; 2 Timothy 4:22. The formula is Pauline and places this epistle in the literary tradition of the Pauline mission. The "all" is deliberately inclusive — the whole congregation, regardless of their spiritual state, is encompassed in the final prayer.

Grace be with you all — may the Divine favor ever rest upon you and among you; and may you receive, from that source of all good, whatsoever is calculated to make you wise, holy, useful, and happy! The single word sums the epistle: the grace that appeared in the Son, was secured by his blood, and is received through faith — this is what the letter has been about, and this is the final gift the author desires for his readers.

Grace be with you all. Romans 16:20. The subscription at the close of the epistle, "written to the Hebrews from Italy by Timothy," like the other subscriptions, is of no authority. The benediction itself is both simple and sufficient: the grace of God in Christ — the grace that the epistle has described, argued for, and applied throughout thirteen chapters — is the one thing needed for every reader in every circumstance.
